title: bug: game reminders not delivered — no cron scheduler configured
description:
## Problem

Game reminders (24h, 2h, 1h before game, and post-game) are never delivered via push notification, even though player join/leave notifications work fine.

## Root cause

The reminder system relies on `POST /api/cron/reminders` being called externally on a schedule. This endpoint:
1. Finds events with games in the reminder window
2. Enqueues push notifications
3. Drains the notification queue
4. Marks reminders as sent

However, there is **no cron scheduler configured** in `fly.toml` or anywhere else. The endpoint exists but is never called, so reminders are never sent.

Player join/leave notifications work because they are triggered synchronously within the API request (enqueue + drain happens inline when a player is added/removed).

## Solution

Add a scheduled machine or external cron to call `POST /api/cron/reminders` every 5-10 minutes.

### Option A: Fly.io Machines (recommended for current setup)

Add a `[processes]` section with a cron worker, or use Fly.io's built-in scheduled machines:

```toml
# fly.toml — add a scheduled machine
[[machines]]
  auto_destroy = true

[machines.schedule]
  cron = "*/5 * * * *"
  command = ["curl", "-X", "POST", "-H", "Authorization: Bearer $CRON_SECRET", "http://localhost:3000/api/cron/reminders"]
```

### Option B: GitHub Actions cron workflow

```yaml
name: Cron - Reminders
on:
  schedule:
    - cron: '*/5 * * * *'
jobs:
  trigger:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - run: |
          curl -X POST \
            -H "Authorization: Bearer ${{ secrets.CRON_SECRET }}" \
            https://convocados.fly.dev/api/cron/reminders
```

### Option C: External cron service (cron-job.org, Upstash QStash, etc.)

Configure an external service to hit `https://convocados.fly.dev/api/cron/reminders` with the `Authorization: Bearer <CRON_SECRET>` header every 5 minutes.

## Notes

- The `CRON_SECRET` env var must be set on Fly.io for the endpoint to accept requests
- The reminder windows are: 22-26h, 1-3h, 30-90min before game time — a 5-minute cron interval ensures no window is missed
- Post-game reminders fire within 4 hours after the game ends

## Related

- #257 (global push subscription)

title: feat: merge logged-in user with anonymous player instead of simple claim
description:
## Problem

The current "It's me" button on the event page allows a logged-in user to claim an anonymous player slot. However, this is a simple reassignment — it only links the anonymous player to the user account.

There are cases where both the anonymous player and the logged-in user already have history (game stats, attendance records, Elo ratings, payment records, etc.). A simple claim loses the anonymous player's history or creates duplicate records.

## Proposed Solution

Add a **merge** flow that:

1. Detects when both the anonymous player and the logged-in user have existing history
2. Offers the user a choice: simple claim (current behavior) vs. merge
3. On merge:
   - Combine game history from both identities
   - Merge attendance records
   - Recalculate Elo ratings based on combined history
   - Consolidate payment records
   - Update all `player.userId` references across events
   - Keep the logged-in user as the canonical identity

## Considerations

- Need to handle conflicts (e.g., both have entries for the same event)
- Should be wrapped in a transaction to avoid partial merges
- May need an admin confirmation step for safety
- Consider showing a preview of what will be merged before executing
- Edge case: what if the anonymous player is claimed by someone else mid-merge?

## Affected Areas

- `src/pages/api/events/[id]/claim-player.ts`
- Player stats and history queries
- Elo rating calculations
- Payment records
- Event log entries

title: feat: automate Play Store deployment via CI
description:
## Summary

Automate Android app deployment to the Google Play Store from CI after each release.

## Prerequisites
- [x] Google Play Developer account approved
- [ ] App listing created in Play Console (screenshots, description, privacy policy)
- [ ] Release signing keystore generated

## Tasks
- [ ] Generate a release signing keystore and store it as GitHub secrets (`KEYSTORE_BASE64`, `KEYSTORE_PASSWORD`, `KEY_ALIAS`, `KEY_PASSWORD`)
- [ ] Create a Google Play service account with upload permissions and store the JSON key as a secret (`PLAY_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_JSON`)
- [ ] Update the Android build to produce a signed AAB (`bundleRelease`) instead of debug APK
- [ ] Add a CI workflow step (or new workflow) to upload the AAB to Play Console using `r0adkll/upload-google-play` or Fastlane `supply`
- [ ] Configure release tracks (internal testing → production promotion)

## Notes
- Google Play requires AAB format (not APK)
- First submission requires manual review and can take several days
- Consider using internal testing track for initial uploads before promoting to production

## Related
- Fix release workflow branch protection issue (github-actions bot cannot push version bump to main)

feat: native Kotlin WearOS app
description:
## Goal
Build a standalone native WearOS app in Kotlin using Jetpack Compose for Wear OS.

## Why native over React Native
- React Native does not run on WearOS — the existing `mobile/src/wear/` code only ran on the phone
- Wear Compose provides first-class round/square screen support, complications, and tiles
- Better battery life and performance than a web/RN bridge approach
- Proper WearOS UX patterns (rotary input, swipe-to-dismiss, always-on display)

## Scope
- **Score tracker** — increment/decrement team scores during a game (offline-first, syncs via phone)
- **Game list** — view upcoming games from the phone companion app
- **Auth** — share OAuth tokens from the phone app via Wearable Data Layer API

## Architecture
- Standalone WearOS module in `wearos/` directory (Kotlin + Gradle)
- Communicates with the Android phone app via `com.google.android.gms:play-services-wearable` Data Layer
- Phone app acts as the data relay to the server
- Offline-first: scores saved locally, synced when phone is reachable

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Score tracker tile/app works without phone connection (offline)
- [ ] Scores sync to server when phone reconnects
- [ ] Game list shows upcoming events from phone
- [ ] Auth tokens shared from phone — no separate login on watch
- [ ] Builds as part of the existing Android release workflow

title: ci: add APK signing to Android release build
description:
## Problem
The `build-android` job in `release.yml` produces an unsigned release APK via `./gradlew assembleRelease`. Android 14+ won't install unsigned APKs from unknown sources.

## Solution
- Generate a release keystore and store it as a GitHub Actions secret (base64-encoded)
- Add a signing step in the workflow that decodes the keystore and signs the APK
- Alternatively, use `bundleRelease` to produce an AAB for Play Store distribution

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] APK is signed with a release keystore
- [ ] Keystore password stored as GitHub secret
- [ ] Signed APK attached to GitHub releases

title: ci: use real google-services.json for FCM in Android builds
description:
## Problem
The `build-android` job in `release.yml` creates a placeholder `google-services.json` with dummy values. This means the APK will compile but Firebase Cloud Messaging (FCM) won't work — push notifications via Expo's push service require a valid FCM configuration.

## Solution
1. Create a Firebase project and download the real `google-services.json`
2. Base64-encode it and store as a GitHub Actions secret (`GOOGLE_SERVICES_JSON`)
3. Decode it in the workflow instead of generating a placeholder

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Real `google-services.json` used in CI builds
- [ ] FCM push notifications work on the built APK
- [ ] Secret is not committed to the repository

title: chore: add stale push token cleanup
description:
## Problem
`AppPushToken` (Expo/FCM) and `PushSubscription` (web push) records accumulate indefinitely. Web push handles 410/404 responses by deleting stale subscriptions reactively, and the new Expo push code removes `DeviceNotRegistered` tokens. However, there's no proactive cleanup for:
- Tokens from users who uninstalled the app without signing out
- Tokens from devices that haven't been used in months
- Web push subscriptions where the browser revoked permission silently

## Solution
Add a cron job (or extend the existing reminders cron) that:
1. Deletes `AppPushToken` records not updated in 90+ days
2. Deletes `PushSubscription` records not used in 90+ days (add a `lastUsedAt` column)
3. Optionally: send a test push to stale tokens and remove those that fail

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Stale tokens are cleaned up periodically
- [ ] Cleanup is tested
- [ ] No impact on active users

title: fix: Dockerfile doesn't account for pnpm workspace
description:
## Problem
The Dockerfile copies `package.json` and `pnpm-lock.yaml` but not `pnpm-workspace.yaml`. Since the project is now a monorepo (root + `mobile/`), the lockfile references workspace packages. The Docker build currently works because it only installs root dependencies, but this is fragile and may break if pnpm enforces workspace consistency.

## Solution
Either:
1. Copy `pnpm-workspace.yaml` into the Docker build context and create a minimal `mobile/package.json` stub, or
2. Add `mobile/` to `.dockerignore` and use `pnpm deploy` to create a standalone install for the server package only

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Docker build is deterministic and doesn't depend on workspace layout assumptions
- [ ] `mobile/` dependencies are not installed in the production image

title: fix: redirectUrls stored as comma-separated instead of JSON
description:
## Problem
In `auth.server.ts:68`, the trusted client's `redirectUrls` is stored as `redirectUrls.join(",")`, producing a comma-separated string. However, the Prisma schema comment says "JSON array of redirect URIs", and better-auth's oidcProvider may expect JSON format.

If better-auth internally parses this as JSON, redirect URL validation will fail for trusted clients with multiple redirect URIs.

## Solution
- Verify what format better-auth expects for `redirectUrls` in the `OauthApplication` table
- If JSON: change `.join(",")` to `JSON.stringify(redirectUrls)`
- If comma-separated: update the schema comment to match

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Format is consistent between storage and consumption
- [ ] Trusted client with multiple redirect URIs works correctly
- [ ] Schema comment matches actual format

## Solution

- [ ] Coverage thresholds still met

title: security: review CSRF posture with checkOrigin disabled
description:
## Problem
`astro.config.mjs` has `security: { checkOrigin: false }` to support OAuth 2.1 endpoints that receive cross-origin requests from mobile apps and API clients. However, this disables CSRF protection for **all** routes, including session-authenticated mutation endpoints like `/api/events/[id]/players`.

better-auth's `trustedOrigins` only protects auth routes, not custom API routes.

## Options
1. Re-enable `checkOrigin: true` and add explicit CSRF bypass only for OAuth endpoints (`/api/auth/...`)
2. Add a custom CSRF token middleware for session-authenticated mutations
3. Validate the `Origin` header manually in API route handlers

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Session-authenticated mutations are protected against CSRF
- [ ] OAuth token/authorize endpoints still work from external clients
- [ ] Tests cover CSRF rejection for cross-origin session requests

title: feat: global push subscription — subscribe once, get notified everywhere
description:
## Problem

Push notifications currently require users to click "Subscribe" on each individual event page. Each `PushSubscription` record is tied to a specific `eventId`. This means:

- Users miss notifications for events they haven't visited yet (e.g. being added as admin)
- The UX doesn't feel like a native app — users expect to subscribe once and get all notifications
- `sendPushToUser()` (added in #255) can only reach devices that already have at least one event subscription

## Proposed Solution

1. **Add a global (account-level) push subscription** — a `PushSubscription` record with `eventId = NULL` (or a dedicated `GlobalPushSubscription` table) created when the user enables push in their notification settings
2. **Auto-subscribe on login** — when a logged-in user grants notification permission, register a global subscription automatically
3. **Update `sendPushToUser()`** — also query global subscriptions (not just event-scoped ones)
4. **Update `sendPushToEvent()`** — for event-level notifications (player joined, reminders), also include global subscriptions from users who are players/admins of that event
5. **Migrate the NotifyButton** — keep it for anonymous/non-logged users, but for logged-in users the global subscription should cover everything

## Impact

This would make the following notifications work even if the user never visited the event page:
- Admin role added/removed (#255)
- Game invites
- Any future account-level notifications (e.g. ownership transfer)

## Considerations

- Backward compatible: existing per-event subscriptions continue to work
- The `PushSubscription` schema already has an optional `userId` field and a `@@index([userId])`
- Need to handle deduplication — a device may have both a global and per-event subscription
- Service worker registration should happen at the app shell level, not just on event pages

title: feat: temporary payment method override for admins
description:
## Summary

When the event owner is not attending a game, a different person may be paying. Admins should be able to set **temporary payment methods** that fully replace the default ones for the current week. The override automatically reverts to the default after the recurring game resets.

## Motivation

Some weeks the owner is not going to the game and someone else will be paying. Rather than changing the default payment methods back and forth every week, admins should be able to set a one-off override that auto-reverts.

## Design

### Schema changes (`EventCost`)

Add two nullable columns:
- `tempPaymentMethods String?` — JSON override, same format as `paymentMethods`
- `tempPaymentDetails String?` — free-text override, same format as `paymentDetails`

### API

**New endpoint: `PUT /api/events/[id]/cost/override`**
- Accepts `{ paymentMethods, paymentDetails }` — sets the temp override
- Auth: owner or admin only
- Validates payment methods using existing `validatePaymentMethods`

**New endpoint: `DELETE /api/events/[id]/cost/override`**
- Clears the temp override (sets both temp fields to null)
- Auth: owner or admin only

**Modified: `GET /api/events/[id]/cost`**
- Returns `effectivePaymentMethods` and `effectivePaymentDetails` — resolved from temp if set, otherwise from default
- Returns `hasOverride: boolean` so the UI knows whether an override is active
- Raw `paymentMethods` and `tempPaymentMethods` still returned for admin editing

### Recurrence reset (`src/pages/api/events/[id]/index.ts`)

During the reset transaction, clear `tempPaymentMethods` and `tempPaymentDetails` on the EventCost. This is the automatic revert.

### UI (`PaymentSection.tsx`)

- **Regular users**: display `effectivePaymentMethods` / `effectivePaymentDetails` — no change in UX
- **Admins** (`canEdit`):
  - "Override payment methods" button (only when cost is already set)
  - When override is active: subtle "Temporary" chip + "Clear override" button
  - Override editor reuses the existing payment method form (type selector + value input)
  - Minimal footprint — collapsible section or small dialog

### i18n (all 6 locales)

New keys:
- `overridePaymentMethods` — "Override payment methods"
- `overridePaymentMethodsDesc` — "Set temporary payment methods for this week. They'll revert to the default after the game resets."
- `clearOverride` — "Clear override"
- `clearOverrideConfirm` — "Revert to default payment methods?"
- `temporaryOverride` — "Temporary"
- `overrideSaved` — "Temporary payment methods saved."
- `overrideCleared` — "Reverted to default payment methods."

## Tests (TDD)

- `PUT /cost/override` sets temp fields; `GET /cost` returns `hasOverride: true` and effective methods reflect the override
- `DELETE /cost/override` clears temp fields; effective methods revert to defaults
- Recurrence reset clears temp override automatically
- Non-admin cannot set override (403)
- Override with invalid payment methods is rejected (400)

## Files to touch

1. `prisma/schema.prisma` — 2 new fields on EventCost
2. `src/pages/api/events/[id]/cost/override.ts` — new endpoint (PUT + DELETE)
3. `src/pages/api/events/[id]/cost.ts` — modify GET response
4. `src/pages/api/events/[id]/index.ts` — clear override on recurrence reset
5. `src/components/PaymentSection.tsx` — admin override UI
6. `src/lib/i18n/{en,pt,es,fr,de,it}.ts` — new keys
7. `src/test/payments.test.ts` — new test cases

## UX notes

- Users see no difference — the payment section looks identical whether it's the default or an override
- Only admins see the override controls
- The override is a full replacement, not additive

title: feat: organizer dashboard for pending actions across past events
description:
## Context

When a recurring event resets, the old game's history moves to the past but pending payments or missing scores from that game may still be unresolved. Currently, the post-game banner only handles the **current** event's state — once the event resets to a future date, the banner disappears and those pending actions are lost from view.

## Problem

Organizers have no centralized place to see and manage pending actions (missing scores, unpaid players) across all their past events. They have to manually visit each event's history page to check.

## Proposed Solution

Create an **organizer dashboard page** (e.g. `/dashboard/actions` or a section in the existing dashboard) that:

1. **Lists all events with pending actions** — events where:
   - The most recent game history has no score recorded
   - There are pending/unpaid player payments
   - The game has ended (dateTime + durationMinutes < now)

2. **Shows a summary per event** — title, date, what's pending (score / payments / both)

3. **Quick actions** — links to the history page (for score entry) and payment section (for payment tracking)

4. **Stacks actions chronologically** — oldest unresolved first, so nothing falls through the cracks

## Technical Notes

- Can reuse the `post-game-status` API endpoint per event, or create a batch endpoint
- Should be scoped to events owned by the authenticated user
- Consider a badge/count on the main dashboard nav to indicate pending actions

## Related

Follow-up from #249 (post-game experience) — review issue 7.

title: feat: post-game experience — score entry, payment tracking, and notifications
description:
## Summary

Improve the post-game experience by surfacing actionable tasks (score entry + payment) at the top of the event page after a game ends, sending notifications to players, and hiding completed events from the active dashboard list.

## Context

Currently, after a game ends:
- There is no prompt to enter scores or settle payments
- No notification is sent to players about payments or next game registration
- Ended events stay in the dashboard indefinitely regardless of completion status

## Requirements

### 1. Game Duration Field
- Add `durationMinutes` (Int) to the `Event` model in Prisma schema
- Default duration per sport:
  | Sport | Default Duration |
  |-------|-----------------|
  | football-5v5 | 60 min |
  | football-7v7 | 70 min |
  | football-11v11 | 90 min |
  | futsal | 60 min |
  | basketball | 48 min |
  | volleyball | 60 min |
  | tennis-singles | 90 min |
  | tennis-doubles | 90 min |
  | padel | 90 min |
  | other | 60 min |
- Add `defaultDurationMinutes` to `SportPreset` in `src/lib/sports.ts`
- Owner can override per event in settings
- "Game ended" = `dateTime + durationMinutes` is in the past

### 2. Post-Game Banner on Event Page
- When the game has ended (dateTime + duration in the past), show a prominent banner at the **top** of the event page (`/events/:id`)
- Banner shows a checklist of pending post-game tasks:
  - [ ] Enter game score (links to score entry / history section)
  - [ ] Complete payments (links to payment section)
- Each item shows a checkmark when completed
- Banner disappears when all tasks are done

### 3. Hide Completed Events from Dashboard
- On `/dashboard`, hide events from the owned/joined active lists when **both**:
  - Score has been entered (GameHistory exists with scoreOne/scoreTwo set)
  - All payments are marked as paid or exempt (no pending payments)
- These events remain accessible via the history page
- The `GET /api/me/games` endpoint needs to be updated to filter out completed past events

### 4. Post-Game Notifications (Cron-Based)
- Add a new `"post_game"` notification type to `NotificationJobType`
- In the cron job (`/api/cron/reminders`), after the game ends (dateTime + duration in the past):
  - Send a push/email notification to all players with accounts
  - Message: "Game ended! Remember to pay and register for the next one"
  - Include link to the event page
  - Deduplicate: only send once per event (use ReminderLog with type `"post_game"`)
- Add `postGameReminderPush` and `postGameReminderEmail` to `NotificationPreferences`
- Add i18n keys for the notification text in all 6 locales (en, pt, es, fr, de, it)

## Implementation Plan (4 PRs)

### PR 1: Duration field + sport defaults
- Prisma migration: add `durationMinutes Int @default(60)` to Event
- Update `SportPreset` with `defaultDurationMinutes`
- Populate `durationMinutes` from sport default on event creation
- Add duration to event settings UI
- Add helper: `isGameEnded(event)` = `dateTime + durationMinutes < now`
- Tests for duration defaults and `isGameEnded` logic

### PR 2: Post-game banner on event page
- New `PostGameBanner` component with checklist (score + payments)
- Fetch post-game status: has score? all payments done?
- Show banner at top of EventPage when game ended + tasks pending
- i18n keys for banner text in all 6 locales
- Tests for banner visibility logic

### PR 3: Hide completed events from dashboard
- Update `GET /api/me/games` to exclude events where:
  - `dateTime + durationMinutes < now` AND
  - GameHistory exists with score AND
  - All payments are paid/exempt (or no cost set)
- Add "completed" filter/section if needed
- Tests for the filtering logic

### PR 4: Post-game notifications
- Add `post_game` to `NotificationJobType`
- Add `postGameReminderPush`/`postGameReminderEmail` to NotificationPreferences
- Prisma migration for new preference fields
- Cron logic: find ended events without post_game reminder sent, enqueue notifications
- Dedup via ReminderLog with type `"post_game"`
- i18n keys f notification text
- Tests for notification sending and deduplication

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Game duration is configurable per event with sport-based defaults
- [ ] Post-game banner appears on event page after game ends
- [ ] Banner shows pending tasks (score + payments) with completion status
- [ ] Banner disappears when all tasks are done
- [ ] Completed events are hidden from dashboard active lists
- [ ] Post-game notification sent to all players after game ends
- [ ] Notification is sent only once per event
- [ ] All changes follow TDD approach
- [ ] i18n strings added to all 6 locales
- [ ] All tests pass, typecheck clean

title: feat: admin ability to delete users
description:
## Problem

There are situations where we need to remove user accounts:
- Account hasn't appeared for a long time
- User was created anonymously but the person later created their own account and wants to use that one instead

## Proposed solution

Add a delete user action to the admin dashboard.

### Behavior on delete
- Hard delete the `User` record
- Existing schema cascade/SetNull rules handle cleanup automatically:
  - `Session`, `Account`, `ApiKey`, `CalendarToken`, `NotificationPreferences`, `AppPushToken`, `PriorityEnrollment`, `PriorityConfirmation`, `EventInvite`, `EventAdmin` → cascade deleted
  - `Event.ownerId` → set to null (events become ownerless)
  - `Player.userId` → set to null (player name is preserved in the roster, just unlinked)
  - `PushSubscription.userId` → set to null
- No reassignment of player records (keep it simple)

### Safety guardrails
- Admin cannot delete themselves
- Confirmation dialog before deletion
- Rate limiting on the DELETE endpoint

## Files to change

- `src/lib/admin.server.ts` — add `deleteUser(userId)` function
- `src/pages/api/admin/users/[id].ts` *(new)* — `DELETE` handler, admin-only
- `src/components/AdminDashboardPage.tsx` — delete icon button per row + confirmation dialog in the user table
- `src/lib/i18n/en.ts` + `pt.ts`, `es.ts`, `fr.ts`, `de.ts`, `it.ts` — new keys (`adminDeleteUser`, `adminDeleteUserConfirm`, `adminDeleteUserSuccess`, `adminDeleteUserError`, `adminCannotDeleteSelf`)
- `src/test/admin.test.ts` — tests for the new `deleteUser` function and API route

title: feat: add Yoga sport option with simplified class management mode
description:
## Summary

Add **Yoga** as a sport option. When selected, it should operate in a simplified "class management" mode — no teams, no ELO, no cost splitting. The focus is purely on managing who can attend the class.

## Motivation

Yoga (and similar solo/class-based activities) don't have teams, scores, or competitive elements. Showing those features creates noise and confusion for organizers running classes rather than team sports.

## Proposed Changes

### 1. Add Yoga to `SPORT_PRESETS` (`src/lib/sports.ts`)

```ts
{ id: "yoga", labelKey: "sportYoga", defaultMaxPlayers: 15 }
```

### 2. Add a `teamsDisabled` flag to sport presets

Introduce a `teamsDisabled?: boolean` field on `SportPreset` to mark sports that don't support teams/extras. Yoga should have `teamsDisabled: true`.

### 3. Disable features in `EventSettingsPage` when sport is team-incompatible

When the event's sport has `teamsDisabled: true`, the following settings should be **visually disabled** with a helper hint explaining why:

- Balanced teams toggle
- ELO enabled toggle
- Allow manual rating toggle
- Split costs toggle

The hint should read something like: _"Not available for Yoga — this sport doesn't use teams or scoring."_

Use MUI `Tooltip` with the hint text on the disabled `FormControlLabel` wrappers.

### 4. Hide team name fields in `CreateEventForm`

When Yoga (or any `teamsDisabled` sport) is selected, hide the "Team names" section in the advanced options accordion.

### 5. Hide teams UI in the event page

When the event sport has `teamsDisabled`, the teams section (randomize button, team picker, etc.) should not be rendered.

### 6. i18n

Add `sportYoga` key to all 6 locale files (`en`, `pt`, `es`, `fr`, `de`, `it`) and a `sportDisabledForSport` hint key.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Yoga appears in the sport dropdown (create form + settings)
- [ ] Selecting Yoga hides team name fields in the create form
- [ ] In event settings, ELO / balanced / manual rating / split costs toggles are disabled with a tooltip hint when sport is Yoga
- [ ] The event page hides the teams section for Yoga events
- [ ] All 6 locales have the new translation keys
- [ ] Tests cover the `teamsDisabled` helper and sport preset lookup

## Notes

- The `teamsDisabled` flag should be generic enough to reuse for future solo/class sports (e.g. swimming, running)
- No schema migration needed — the `sport` field already stores the sport id as a string

title: feat: zero-downtime migration from SQLite to PostgreSQL for existing instances
description:
## Overview

Once PostgreSQL support lands (#236), existing self-hosted instances running SQLite need a safe, documented path to migrate their data without losing anything and without taking the app offline for more than a few minutes.

## Goals

- No data loss
- Minimal downtime (target: < 5 minutes of read-only mode)
- Reversible — easy rollback if something goes wrong
- Works for anyone running the Docker Compose setup or a bare-metal install

## Migration Strategy

The recommended approach is a **dump-and-restore with a brief maintenance window**:

### Phase 1 — Prepare (no downtime)

1. Spin up the new Postgres container alongside the running app (still on SQLite)
2. Run `prisma migrate deploy` against Postgres to create the schema (empty DB)
3. Verify connectivity: `psql $DATABASE_URL -c '\dt'`

### Phase 2 — Export from SQLite

Use [pgloader](https://pgloader.io/) or a custom script to stream data from SQLite into Postgres.

**Option A — pgloader (recommended)**

```bash
pgloader sqlite:///data/db.sqlite postgresql://convocados:changeme@localhost:5432/convocados
```

pgloader handles type coercions (e.g. SQLite `INTEGER` → Postgres `BIGINT`, booleans, datetimes) automatically.

**Option B — Prisma seed script**

For smaller datasets, export via `prisma db pull` + a custom TypeScript script that reads from the SQLite client and writes to the Postgres client in batches.

### Phase 3 — Cutover (brief maintenance window)

1. Put the app in maintenance mode (or stop the container)
2. Re-run the export to catch any writes that happened since Phase 2
3. Update `DATABASE_URL` to point to Postgres
4. Restart the app — `prisma migrate deploy` runs on startup and is a no-op (schema already applied)
5. Smoke-test: verify event count, user count, latest records match

### Phase 4 — Cleanup

- Keep the SQLite file as a backup for at least 7 days
- Remove Litestream config if no longer needed
- Update monitoring/alerts to point at Postgres

## Deliverables

### Migration script

A shell script at `scripts/migrate-sqlite-to-postgres.sh`:

```sh
#!/bin/sh
# Usage: SQLITE_PATH=/data/db.sqlite POSTGRES_URL=postgresql://... ./scripts/migrate-sqlite-to-postgres.sh
set -e

echo "[migrate] Checking pgloader..."
command -v pgloader >/dev/null 2>&1 || { echo "pgloader not found. Install: https://pgloader.io"; exit 1; }

echo "[migrate] Exporting SQLite → Postgres..."
pgloader $SQLITE_PATH $POSTGRES_URL

echo "[migrate] Done. Verify row counts before switching DATABASE_URL."
```

### Verification queries

A `scripts/verify-migration.sql` that checks row counts across all tables match between the two databases.

### Documentation

A `docs/migrate-sqlite-to-postgres.md` guide covering:
- Prerequisites (pgloader, access to both DBs)
- Step-by-step with exact commands
- Rollback procedure
- Common pitfalls (boolean coercion, JSON strings, datetime timezone handling)

## Known Pitfalls to Document

| Issue | Detail |
|---|---|
| Booleans | SQLite stores as `0`/`1`; pgloader converts automatically but verify |
| JSON strings | Columns like `teamsSnapshot`, `scopes`, `details` are `TEXT` in SQLite — after migration to Postgres `Json` type, values must be valid JSON |
| Datetimes | SQLite stores as ISO strings; Postgres uses `timestamptz` — verify timezone handling |
| `cuid()` IDs | String PKs — no issue, copy as-is |
| Unique constraints | pgloader respects them; duplicate rows (if any) will fail — run a pre-check |

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `scripts/migrate-sqlite-to-postgres.sh` works end-to-end on a real SQLite database
- [ ] `scripts/verify-migration.sql` confirms row counts match for all tables
- [ ] `docs/migrate-sqlite-to-postgres.md` documents the full procedure including rollback
- [ ] Migration tested on a copy of a production-like SQLite database
- [ ] Total downtime during cutover is < 5 minutes

## Dependencies

- Requires #236 (PostgreSQL + Docker Compose support) to be merged first

title: feat: add PostgreSQL support with Docker Compose for easy self-hosting
description:
## Overview

The current SQLite setup has limitations for production deployments: no connection pooling, single-writer bottleneck, and Litestream adds operational complexity. This issue tracks adding first-class PostgreSQL support with a simple `docker-compose.yml` that lets anyone spin up or self-host Convocados with a single command.

## Motivation

- SQLite WAL mode limits concurrent writes and doesn't scale horizontally
- Litestream replication is a workaround, not a proper HA solution
- PostgreSQL unlocks connection pooling, better concurrency, native JSON operators, and standard managed hosting (Supabase, Neon, Railway, Render, etc.)
- A single `docker compose up` should be enough to run the full stack locally or on a VPS

## Proposed Changes

### 1. Prisma schema — support both providers

Update `prisma/schema.prisma` to use PostgreSQL as the primary provider. Keep SQLite available for local dev/tests via an env var so CI stays fast.

```prisma
datasource db {
  provider = env("DATABASE_PROVIDER") // "postgresql" | "sqlite"
  url      = env("DATABASE_URL")
}
```

Or maintain two schema files (`schema.sqlite.prisma` / `schema.postgresql.prisma`) and select at build time.

> **Note:** Fields using `String` for JSON blobs (e.g. `teamsSnapshot`, `events`, `scopes`, `details`) should be migrated to native `Json` type in Postgres for better querying.

### 2. New `docker-compose.yml`

A single file at the repo root to run the full stack:

```yaml
volumes:
  convocados-db:

services:
  db:
    image: postgres:17-alpine
    environment:
      POSTGRES_DB: convocados
      POSTGRES_USER: convocados
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: ${POSTGRES_PASSWORD:-changeme}
    volumes:
      - convocados-db:/var/lib/postgresql/data
    restart: unless-stopped
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U convocados"]
      interval: 10s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 5

  app:
    image: ghcr.io/cabeda/convocados:latest
    ports:
      - "3000:3000"
    environment:
      DATABASE_URL: postgresql://convocados:${POSTGRES_PASSWORD:-changeme}@db:5432/convocados
      # Copy remaining vars from .env.example
    depends_on:
      db:
        condition: service_healthy
    restart: unless-stopped
```

### 3. Update `Dockerfile`

- Remove `openssl` workaround (only needed for SQLite Prisma engine on Alpine)
- Add `postgresql-client` for healthcheck scripts if needed
- Ensure `prisma generate` targets the PostgreSQL engine

### 4. Update `scripts/start.sh`

- Remove the SQLite-specific failed-migration resolution logic (not needed for Postgres)
- Keep `prisma migrate deploy` — works identically for Postgres

### 5. Update `.env.example`

```env
# PostgreSQL (recommended for production)
DATABASE_URL=postgresql://convocados:changeme@localhost:5432/convocados

# SQLite (local dev / tests only)
# DATABASE_URL=file:./dev.db
```

### 6. Remove / make Litestream optional

`litestream.yml` is SQLite-specific. With Postgres, backups are handled by `pg_dump` or managed provider snapshots. The file can be removed or kept behind a feature flag for users who still want SQLite.

### 7. CI / tests

Tests currently use `test.db` (SQLite). Options:
- **Keep SQLite for unit tests** (fast, no Docker needed in CI) — use `DATABASE_PROVIDER=sqlite`
- **Add a Postgres service in CI** for integration tests via GitHub Actions `services:`

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `docker compose up` starts the app + Postgres and the app is reachable at `http://localhost:3000`
- [ ] `prisma migrate deploy` runs cleanly against a fresh Postgres database
- [ ] All existing tests pass (SQLite path kept for unit tests)
- [ ] `.env.example` documents both connection strings
- [ ] `README.md` updated with self-hosting instructions using Docker Compose
- [ ] JSON blob columns (`teamsSnapshot`, `events`, `scopes`, etc.) migrated to native `Json` type in Postgres schema

## References

- Readeck's compose setup as inspiration: https://readeck.org/en/docs/compose
- Prisma multi-provider docs: https://www.prisma.io/docs/orm/reference/prisma-schema-reference#provider

title: OAuth 2.1: Unify and complete OpenAPI spec for mobile consumption
description:
## Summary

Make the API truly mobile-friendly by ensuring consistent JSON responses, proper error formats, and complete OpenAPI documentation including the new OAuth 2.1 security scheme.

## Context

The current OpenAPI spec (`src/lib/openapi.ts`) covers most endpoints but is missing:
- OAuth 2.1 security scheme definition
- Several endpoint groups (costs, payments, priority, access control, admin, API keys)
- Standardized error response schemas
- Pagination parameters on list endpoints

A complete spec allows mobile developers to auto-generate typed API clients (e.g., using OpenAPI Generator for Kotlin/Swift).

## Tasks

- [ ] Update `src/lib/openapi.ts` to include:
  - OAuth 2.1 security scheme (`oauth2` with authorization code flow + PKCE)
  - API key security scheme (`apiKey` in header)
  - All scopes in the security scheme definition
  - `securitySchemes` section in `components`
- [ ] Add missing endpoint documentation:
  - Cost/payment endpoints (`/api/events/{id}/cost`, `/api/events/{id}/split-costs`, `/api/events/{id}/payments`)
  - Priority enrollment endpoints
  - Access control endpoints (`/api/events/{id}/access`, `/api/events/{id}/access/invites`)
  - Admin endpoints
  - API key management (`/api/me/api-keys`)
  - Notification preferences
  - User stats
  - Data export
- [ ] Standardize error response format across all endpoints:
  ```json
  { "error": "string", "code": "string (optional)", "details": "object (optional)" }
  ```
- [ ] Add pagination support to list endpoints (`?page=1&limit=20`) where missing
- [ ] Ensure all mutation endpoints return the updated resource (not just `{ ok: true }`)
- [ ] Publish the OpenAPI spec version matching the app version from `package.json`
- [ ] Validate the spec against the OpenAPI 3.1 schema in tests

## Acceptance Criteria

- OpenAPI spec validates against the 3.1 schema
- All endpoints are documented with request/response schemas
- A mobile developer can generate a typed API client from the spec
- OAuth 2.1 and API key auth are both documented as security schemes

## Implementation Order

This is **issue 8 of 10** in the OAuth 2.1 epic.

title: OAuth 2.1: Consent screen UI
description:
## Summary

Build a consent page where users approve or deny third-party app access to their Convocados data. First-party apps (like the official Android app) should skip this screen.

## Context

When a third-party app initiates the OAuth flow, the user must see what data the app is requesting and explicitly approve it. This is a standard OAuth requirement. The consent decision should be remembered so users are not asked repeatedly.

## Tasks

- [ ] Create `/oauth/consent` page (Astro + React)
- [ ] Display:
  - Client name and logo (from `OAuthClient.clientName`, `logoUri`)
  - Requested scopes in human-readable form (e.g., "View your events", "Manage players")
  - Links to client privacy policy and ToS (`policyUri`, `tosUri`)
- [ ] "Allow" and "Deny" buttons
- [ ] Remember consent per user+client+scopes (stored in `OAuthConsent` table) so users are not asked repeatedly
- [ ] Allow trusted/first-party clients (e.g., the official Android app) to skip consent via `trustedClients` config in the oidcProvider plugin
- [ ] i18n: add consent screen strings to all 6 locales:
  - `en.ts`, `pt.ts`, `es.ts`, `fr.ts`, `de.ts`, `it.ts`
- [ ] Responsive design — must work well in mobile WebView (Android app will open this in a browser/custom tab)
- [ ] Write tests for consent flow, consent persistence, and trusted client bypass

## Acceptance Criteria

- Third-party apps trigger the consent screen
- First-party Android app skips consent
- Consent is remembered across sessions
- Consent screen is translated in all 6 locales
- Works well in mobile WebView / Custom Chrome Tab

## Implementation Order

This is **issue 6 of 10** in the OAuth 2.1 epic. Depends on the oidcProvider plugin and scopes.

title: OAuth 2.1: Android app integration guide and example
description:
## Summary

Provide a reference implementation guide and documentation for an Android app integrating with Convocados via OAuth 2.1 with dynamic client registration.

## Context

The goal is to make it trivial for a developer to build an Android app that:
1. Self-registers as an OAuth client on first launch
2. Authenticates users via the standard OAuth 2.1 authorization code flow with PKCE
3. Calls the Convocados API with bearer tokens
4. Handles token refresh transparently

The recommended Android library is [AppAuth-Android](https://github.com/openid/AppAuth-Android), which is the reference implementation of OAuth 2.0 / OIDC for Android.

## Tasks

- [ ] Write a developer guide (in `docs/` or the project wiki) covering:
  1. **Discovery**: Fetch `/.well-known/openid-configuration` to get all endpoint URLs
  2. **Dynamic Client Registration**: `POST /oauth2/register` with app metadata
     - Store `client_id` in Android SharedPreferences / EncryptedSharedPreferences
     - Handle the case where the client was already registered (idempotency)
  3. **Authorization Code Flow with PKCE** using AppAuth-Android:
     - Generate `code_verifier` and `code_challenge` (S256)
     - Open authorization URL in Custom Chrome Tab
     - Handle redirect back to app via custom URI scheme (`com.convocados.app://callback`)
     - Exchange code for tokens
  4. **Token Storage**: Store tokens in Android Keystore / EncryptedSharedPreferences
  5. **API Calls**: Add `Authorization: Bearer <access_token>` header to all requests
  6. **Token Refresh**: Use AppAuth's built-in token refresh, handle `401` responses
  7. **Logout / Token Revocation**: Call the revocation endpoint
- [ ] Document custom URI scheme setup in `AndroidManifest.xml`:
  ```xml
  <intent-filter>
    <action android:name="android.intent.action.VIEW" />
    <category android:name="android.intent.category.DEFAULT" />
    <category android:name="android.intent.category.BROWSABLE" />
    <data android:scheme="com.convocados.app" android:host="callback" />
  </intent-filter>
  ```
- [ ] Provide a minimal Kotlin code example or link to a template repo
- [ ] Document error handling: network errors, token expiry, revoked access
- [ ] Add a "Mobile Integration" section to the OpenAPI spec description

## Acceptance Criteria

- A developer can follow the guide to build a working Android app from scratch
- The guide covers registration, auth, API calls, token refresh, and revocation
- Code examples are in Kotlin and use AppAuth-Android

## Implementation Order

This is **issue 10 of 10** in the OAuth 2.1 epic. Can be written in parallel with implementation work.

title: OAuth 2.1: Token introspection and revocation endpoints
description:
## Summary

Implement RFC 7662 (Token Introspection) and RFC 7009 (Token Revocation) for proper token lifecycle management. These are essential for mobile apps to check token validity and for users to revoke app access.

## Tasks

- [ ] Enable introspection and revocation in the oidcProvider config
- [ ] `POST /api/auth/oauth2/introspect` — returns token metadata:
  - `active` (boolean)
  - `scope`, `exp`, `sub`, `client_id`, `token_type`
- [ ] `POST /api/auth/oauth2/revoke` — revokes access or refresh tokens
- [ ] Ensure revoked tokens are immediately rejected on subsequent API calls
- [ ] Add a user-facing "Connected Apps" page where users can see which apps have access and revoke them
- [ ] Write tests for:
  - Introspection of valid tokens
  - Introspection of expired tokens
  - Introspection of revoked tokens
  - Revocation of access tokens
  - Revocation of refresh tokens

## Acceptance Criteria

- Introspection returns correct `active: true/false` status
- Revoked tokens are rejected within the same request cycle
- Users can revoke app access from their account settings

## Implementation Order

This is **issue 7 of 10** in the OAuth 2.1 epic. Depends on the oidcProvider plugin.

title: OAuth 2.1: E2E test — Full OAuth flow from client registration to API call
description:
## Summary

End-to-end integration test that validates the entire OAuth 2.1 flow as a mobile client would experience it. This is the final validation that everything works together.

## Tasks

- [ ] Write a Vitest integration test covering the full flow:
  1. Register a new client via dynamic registration (`POST /oauth2/register`)
  2. Initiate authorization code flow with PKCE (generate `code_verifier` + `code_challenge`)
  3. Simulate user login and consent approval
  4. Exchange authorization code for tokens (`POST /oauth2/token`)
  5. Verify access token contains correct scopes
  6. Call a protected API endpoint with the access token (e.g., `GET /api/me/games`)
  7. Refresh the token using the refresh token
  8. Call the API again with the new access token
  9. Revoke the token
  10. Verify the revoked token is rejected with `401`
- [ ] Test error cases:
  - Invalid PKCE verifier → token exchange fails
  - Expired authorization code → token exchange fails
  - Reused authorization code → token exchange fails
  - Missing scope → API returns `403`
  - Revoked token → API returns `401`
  - Invalid redirect URI during registration → registration fails
- [ ] Mock the user interaction (login/consent) or use a test helper that bypasses the UI
- [ ] Ensure test cleans up after itself (delete registered client, tokens, etc.)

## Acceptance Criteria

- Test passes in CI (`npm run test`)
- Covers the happy path and all key error cases listed above
- Test is self-contained and does not depend on external services

## Implementation Order

This is **issue 9 of 10** in the OAuth 2.1 epic. Should be written after all other implementation issues are complete.

title: OAuth 2.1: Rate limiting and security hardening
description:
## Summary

Protect OAuth endpoints against abuse with rate limiting, short-lived codes, token rotation, and audit logging.

## Context

OAuth endpoints are high-value targets. The token endpoint can be brute-forced, the registration endpoint can be abused for mass client creation, and authorization codes must be single-use. This issue covers all security hardening before the OAuth feature goes live.

## Tasks

- [ ] Rate-limit `/oauth2/token` — prevent brute-force on authorization codes (e.g., 20 req/min per IP)
- [ ] Rate-limit `/oauth2/register` — prevent mass client registration (e.g., 5 req/hour per IP)
- [ ] Rate-limit `/oauth2/authorize` per IP (e.g., 30 req/min)
- [ ] Enforce short expiry on authorization codes (10 minutes max, single-use)
- [ ] Enforce access token expiry (1 hour recommended for mobile)
- [ ] Enforce refresh token rotation (new refresh token on each use, old one invalidated)
- [ ] Add CORS headers for the OAuth endpoints (needed for SPA/mobile web views)
- [ ] Log all OAuth events to an audit trail:
  - Client registration
  - Token issuance
  - Token refresh
  - Token revocation
  - Failed authentication attempts
  - Consider a new `OAuthAuditLog` table or reuse `EventLog`
- [ ] Write tests for rate limiting, code reuse rejection, and token rotation

## Acceptance Criteria

- Authorization codes are single-use and expire in ≤10 minutes
- Refresh token rotation is enforced
- Rate limits return `429 Too Many Requests`
- All OAuth events are logged for audit purposes
- CORS is properly configured for OAuth endpoints

## Implementation Order

This is **issue 5 of 10** in the OAuth 2.1 epic. Should be completed before going live.

title: OAuth 2.1: Add oidcProvider plugin to better-auth
description:
## Summary

Enable Convocados as an OAuth 2.1 authorization server using the `better-auth` OIDC Provider plugin. This is the core foundation for all third-party and mobile app integrations.

## Context

`better-auth` has an official `oidcProvider` plugin that supports OAuth 2.1 with mandatory PKCE, standard endpoints, and dynamic client registration. Since we already use `better-auth`, this is the natural integration path — no need for a second auth library.

Current auth setup (`src/lib/auth.server.ts`):
- Email/password with verification
- Google social login
- Magic links
- Session-based auth via better-auth
- API keys (`cvk_` prefix) for programmatic access

## Tasks

- [ ] Install `@better-auth/oauth-provider` (or use built-in `oidcProvider` from `better-auth/plugins`)
- [ ] Add the `oidcProvider()` plugin to `src/lib/auth.server.ts` config
- [ ] Configure `loginPage`, consent page URL, and trusted clients
- [ ] Ensure PKCE is mandatory (OAuth 2.1 requirement)
- [ ] Verify the following endpoints are mounted automatically:
  - `GET /api/auth/oauth2/authorize`
  - `POST /api/auth/oauth2/token`
  - `GET /api/auth/oauth2/userinfo`
  - `GET /.well-known/openid-configuration`
  - `GET /.well-known/oauth-authorization-server`
- [ ] Add environment variables for issuer URL configuration
- [ ] Configure refresh token rotation
- [ ] Write tests for token issuance, PKCE enforcement, and token refresh

## Acceptance Criteria

- `/.well-known/openid-configuration` returns valid discovery document
- Authorization code flow with PKCE works end-to-end
- Refresh token rotation is enabled
- Existing session auth and API key auth continue to work unchanged

## Implementation Order

This is **issue 2 of 10** in the OAuth 2.1 epic. Depends on the DB schema issue.

title: OAuth 2.1: Define and enforce API scopes
description:
## Summary

Define a granular scope system for OAuth tokens that maps to the existing API surface. Extend the current `API_SCOPES` and unify API key + OAuth token authentication.

## Context

Current scopes in `src/lib/apiKey.server.ts`:
- `read:events`, `write:events`, `manage:players`, `create:events`

These need to be expanded to cover the full API surface and work for both API keys and OAuth tokens.

## Tasks

- [ ] Extend scope definitions (in `src/lib/apiKey.server.ts` or a new `src/lib/scopes.ts`):
  - `read:profile` — read own user profile
  - `read:events` — list and view events (exists)
  - `write:events` — modify event settings (exists)
  - `create:events` — create new events (exists)
  - `manage:players` — add/remove players (exists)
  - `read:ratings` — view ELO ratings
  - `read:history` — view game history
  - `manage:teams` — randomize/assign teams
  - `manage:webhooks` — CRUD webhooks
  - `manage:push` — push subscription management
  - `read:calendar` — calendar feed access
  - `manage:payments` — cost/payment operations
- [ ] Create a unified `authenticateRequest()` middleware that:
  - Checks for session auth (better-auth)
  - Checks for API key auth (`Bearer cvk_...`)
  - Checks for OAuth bearer token (`Bearer ...`)
  - Returns a normalized auth context with `userId`, `scopes`, and `authMethod`
- [ ] Update all API routes to use the unified auth + scope check
- [ ] Write tests for scope enforcement (token with `read:events` cannot call `POST /api/events`)

## Acceptance Criteria

- OAuth tokens are validated and scoped on every protected endpoint
- Existing API key auth continues to work unchanged
- Existing session auth continues to work unchanged
- Scope mismatch returns `403 Forbidden` with a clear error message
- All three auth methods (session, API key, OAuth token) are handled by a single function

## Implementation Order

This is **issue 4 of 10** in the OAuth 2.1 epic. Depends on the oidcProvider plugin setup.

title: OAuth 2.1: Dynamic Client Registration (RFC 7591)
description:
## Summary

Allow mobile apps and third-party integrations to register themselves as OAuth clients programmatically, without manual setup. This is critical for the Android app use case — the app registers itself on first launch.

## Context

RFC 7591 defines a standard protocol for clients to register with an authorization server. The `better-auth` oidcProvider plugin supports this natively. For mobile apps, this means:
1. App launches for the first time
2. App calls `POST /api/auth/oauth2/register` with its metadata
3. Server returns `client_id` (and optionally `client_secret`)
4. App stores credentials and uses them for all future OAuth flows

## Tasks

- [ ] Enable dynamic registration in the `oidcProvider` plugin config
- [ ] Decide on registration policy:
  - Open registration (any app can register) — suitable for a public API
  - OR protected registration (requires an Initial Access Token) — more secure
  - **Recommendation:** start with open registration, add IAT protection later
- [ ] Ensure registered clients are persisted in the `OAuthClient` table
- [ ] Return `client_id`, `client_secret` (for confidential clients), and `registration_access_token` per RFC 7591
- [ ] Support RFC 7592 (Registration Management) for client metadata updates/deletion
- [ ] Validate `redirect_uris`:
  - Enforce HTTPS for production web clients
  - Allow custom URI schemes for mobile apps (e.g., `com.convocados.app://callback`)
  - Allow `http://localhost` for development
- [ ] Rate-limit the registration endpoint
- [ ] Write tests for: successful registration, invalid redirect URIs, duplicate registration, rate limiting

## Acceptance Criteria

- `POST /api/auth/oauth2/register` creates a new client and returns credentials
- Public clients (mobile apps) can register without a `client_secret`
- Custom URI schemes are accepted for native app redirects
- Registration endpoint is rate-limited

## Implementation Order

This is **issue 3 of 10** in the OAuth 2.1 epic. Depends on the oidcProvider plugin setup.

title: OAuth 2.1: Database schema for OAuth clients and tokens
description:
## Summary

Add Prisma models to persist OAuth clients (registered via dynamic registration or manually) and authorization grants/tokens. This is the foundation for the OAuth 2.1 authorization server.

## Context

Convocados currently uses `better-auth` with session-based auth and API keys (`cvk_` prefix). To support OAuth 2.1 for mobile apps and third-party integrations, we need database tables for OAuth clients, authorization codes, tokens, and consent records.

## Tasks

- [ ] Check if `better-auth` oidcProvider plugin auto-manages tables via its Prisma adapter — if so, align with its expected schema rather than creating custom models
- [ ] Add `OAuthClient` model:
  - `id`, `clientId`, `clientSecret` (hashed, nullable for public clients), `clientName`, `redirectUris` (JSON), `grantTypes` (JSON), `responseTypes` (JSON), `tokenEndpointAuthMethod`, `scope`, `logoUri`, `clientUri`, `policyUri`, `tosUri`, `softwareId`, `softwareVersion`, `registrationAccessToken` (hashed), `createdAt`, `updatedAt`
- [ ] Add `OAuthAuthorizationCode` model:
  - `id`, `code`, `clientId`, `userId`, `redirectUri`, `scope`, `codeChallenge`, `codeChallengeMethod`, `expiresAt`, `createdAt`
- [ ] Add `OAuthToken` model:
  - `id`, `accessToken` (hashed), `refreshToken` (hashed), `clientId`, `userId`, `scope`, `accessTokenExpiresAt`, `refreshTokenExpiresAt`, `createdAt`
- [ ] Add `OAuthConsent` model:
  - `id`, `userId`, `clientId`, `scopes` (JSON), `createdAt`
- [ ] Run `prisma migrate dev` and verify migration runs cleanly on SQLite
- [ ] Write tests for model creation and relationships

## Acceptance Criteria

- Migration runs cleanly on SQLite
- Models are compatible with the better-auth OIDC adapter
- All relations and indexes are properly defined

## Implementation Order

This is **issue 1 of 10** in the OAuth 2.1 epic. Must be completed before the oidcProvider plugin setup.

title: feat: Playtomic integration — auto-create ownerless public games from open court slots
description:
## Overview

Integrate with Playtomic to automatically discover open court slots and create ownerless public games for them. Players can join without needing to book or manage anything — the app surfaces the opportunity, Playtomic handles the booking.

## ⚠️ Pre-requisites (must resolve before implementation)

- [ ] Confirm existing Playtomic API integration covers slot availability polling
- [ ] Decide on liability disclaimer: app does not guarantee the court is booked

## How It Works

1. A background job polls Playtomic for open slots by sport/location
2. For each open slot, create a `public` + `ownerless` event in Convocados
3. Event links back to Playtomic for the actual court booking
4. Players join via Convocados; someone in the group books on Playtomic separately

## Data Model Changes

- `ownerId` nullable on `Event` (ownerless games)
- `externalSource` field (e.g. `playtomic`) + `externalId` for deduplication
- `externalBookingUrl` to link back to Playtomic slot
- `autoCreated` boolean flag for filtering/display purposes
- `bookingConfirmedBy` player ID — set when a player claims and confirms the booking
- `bookingConfirmationRef` string — Playtomic booking ID or reference submitted by the booker
- `courtConfirmed` boolean — flipped to true once a booking reference is submitted

## Event Lifecycle

- Auto-created events are hidden until at least 1 player joins (avoid ghost game flood)
- Auto-expire if no player joins within a configurable window (e.g. 6h before slot)
- Deleted/cancelled on Playtomic side → mark as cancelled in Convocados
- When the game fills up, prompt players: "Someone needs to book this court — who's doing it?"
- One player claims the booker role; all other players see who claimed it
- Booker submits their Playtomic booking reference (booking ID or confirmation number)
- Event is marked as "Court confirmed ✓" and all joined players are notified
- If no booking reference is submitted X hours before the game, all players are warned

## Anti-ghosting System

- Login required to join any public game
- Strike system: 2 no-shows within 60 days → 30-day ban from joining public games
- Late cancellation (< 2h before game) counts as a no-show
- Confirmation required 24h before game; unconfirmed spot is freed
- Minimum profile completeness to join: display name + at least 1 past game or verified email

## Player Transparency (Reliability Profile)

Visible to anyone in the same game:
- Attendance rate (e.g. "attended 8/10 games")
- No-show count in last 60 days
- Late cancellation rate (tracked separately from no-shows)
- "New player" badge for zero game history — neutral signal, sets expectations

Private (never shown):
- Full game history (which events, when, with whom)
- Contact info

Notes:
- Only no-shows and late cancellations count against reliability — legitimate cancellations above the 2h window do not
- Players can optionally add a note when cancelling

## UX Considerations

- Clear label on auto-created games: "Open court on Playtomic — no organizer"
- Discovery page filters: hide auto-created games by default, opt-in to see them
- Notify joined players if the slot disappears on Playtomic
- Show booking confirmation status prominently on the event page (unconfirmed / claimed by X / confirmed ✓)

## Risks

- Booking reference is not cryptographically verified — a bad actor could submit a fake reference
- Court may get booked by someone else on Playtomic after players join here
- High event volume needs aggressive filtering/hiding to keep UX clean

## Related

Closes / extends #210

title: feat: public games discovery and join flow
description:
## Overview

Allow event organizers to open their games to the public, and let solo players discover and join existing games near them.

## User Stories

- As an organizer, I want to mark my event as public so strangers can fill empty spots
- As a solo player, I want to find and join pickup games near me without having a group

## Required Changes

### Data model
- Add `isPublic` boolean flag to `Event` (opt-in, default false)
- Add `skillLevel` field to player profiles (e.g. beginner / intermediate / advanced)
- Add `noShowCount`, `lateCancelCount`, and `attendedCount` to player history

### API / backend
- Filter public events on the existing public events endpoint
- Enforce confirmation step: players must confirm attendance 24h before the game; unconfirmed spots are freed automatically
- Only count no-shows for unconfirmed absences; legitimate cancellations above the 2h window are tracked separately

### UI
- Opt-in toggle on event creation/edit form
- Skill level filter on the public events discovery page (map view already exists)
- Confirmation prompt/reminder for players who joined a public game

## Player Transparency (Reliability Profile)

Visible to anyone in the same game:
- Attendance rate (e.g. "attended 8/10 games")
- No-show count in last 60 days
- Late cancellation rate (< 2h before game, tracked separately from no-shows)
- "New player" badge for players with zero game history — neutral signal, sets expectations

Private (never shown):
- Full game history (which events, when, with whom)
- Contact info

Notes:
- Only no-shows and late cancellations count against reliability — legitimate cancellations above the 2h window do not
- Players can optionally add a note when cancelling (not required)

## Out of Scope (for now)
- Verified booking badge (proof of field reservation)
- Post-game rating system
- Automated field availability checking

## Risks / Notes
- Organizer assumes responsibility for the field booking — no booking verification is done by the app
- Trust/safety is opt-in by design; organizers choose to open their game

title: ui: Add proper empty states across the app
description:
## Summary
Add designed empty states with helpful CTAs for all screens that can be empty.

## Current State
Several screens likely show blank content or a generic message when there's no data. Proper empty states with illustrations and clear CTAs make the app feel polished and guide users.

## Screens to Cover
- **Dashboard** (no games yet): Show "Create your first game" CTA with a brief explanation
- **Event player list** (no players signed up): "Share this game to invite players" with share button
- **Game history** (no games played): "Your game history will appear here after your first match"
- **Rankings** (no ratings yet): "Ratings will be calculated after games are recorded with scores"
- **Attendance stats** (no data): "Attendance tracking starts after the first game"
- **Public games** (no public games): "No public games right now. Create one!"

## Design Guidelines
- Use a relevant icon or simple illustration
- Short, friendly copy explaining what will appear here
- A primary action button (create game, share link, etc.)
- Keep it consistent across all empty states

## Priority
Low-medium — polish that improves perceived quality.

title: ui: Improve GameCard with progress bar and quick actions
description:
## Summary
Enhance the dashboard `GameCard` component with visual progress indicators and quick-action buttons.

## Current State
`src/components/GameCard.tsx` shows: title, location, date, and a player count chip (e.g. "7/10"). It's functional but minimal compared to competitors.

## Proposed Improvements

### Visual progress bar for spots
Replace or supplement the "7/10" chip with a visual progress bar:
```
[███████░░░] 7/10 spots filled
```
- Green when plenty of spots, yellow when almost full, red/orange when full
- More immediately scannable than reading numbers

### Next game countdown
For upcoming games, show a prominent countdown:
- "In 2 days" or "Tomorrow at 20h" or "In 3 hours"
- Helps users quickly identify which game is next

### Quick-action buttons
Add inline actions without requiring navigation:
- "Join" / "Leave" button directly on the card (for games the user hasn't joined yet)
- Reduces friction — one tap instead of navigate → scroll → add name → submit

## Priority
Medium — small polish that improves daily UX for returning users.

title: ui: Mobile-first UX audit and polish
description:
## Summary
Conduct a mobile-first UX audit to ensure the app feels native-quality on phones, since the Portuguese amateur football market is 95%+ mobile.

## Areas to Audit

### Touch targets
- Ensure all interactive elements are at least 48x48px (Material Design guideline)
- Check spacing between tappable items in player lists, buttons, chips

### Bottom sheets instead of dialogs
- On mobile, dialogs can feel jarring. Consider using bottom sheets (MUI Drawer with `anchor="bottom"`) for:
  - Add player form
  - Score entry
  - Share options
  - Confirmation prompts

### Swipe gestures
- Swipe-to-remove on player list items (instead of requiring tap → confirm dialog)
- Swipe between tabs if tab navigation is added to EventPage

### Sticky headers
- On long pages (EventPage, HistoryPage), keep the header/title sticky so users always know where they are
- Consider a floating action button for the primary action (add player, record score)

### Performance
- Check for unnecessary re-renders on the 1600-line EventPage
- Lazy load sections below the fold (history, payments)

## Priority
Medium — critical for the target market (mobile-first Portuguese users).

title: ui: Break up EventPage.tsx into smaller sub-components
description:
## Summary
Refactor `EventPage.tsx` (1600+ lines) into focused sub-components for better maintainability and a cleaner mobile UX.

## Current State
`src/components/EventPage.tsx` is a single 1600+ line file handling: event header, player list, bench, team picker, score entry, payments, notifications, settings access, share, countdown, and more. This makes it hard to maintain and likely overwhelming on mobile.

## Proposed Refactor
Extract into focused components:
- `EventHeader.tsx` — title, date, location, countdown, sport badge
- `PlayerList.tsx` — active players with drag-to-reorder, add player form
- `BenchSection.tsx` — waitlisted players
- `EventActions.tsx` — share, shuffle, history, settings, calendar buttons
- `ScoreEntry.tsx` — score input and submission

## UX Improvement
Consider organizing sections with tabs or a segmented control on mobile:
- **Players** tab (player list + bench)
- **Teams** tab (team picker + shuffle)
- **Game** tab (scores + history link)
- **Costs** tab (payment section)

This matches how competitors organize their game screens — focused views instead of one long scroll.

## Priority
Medium — improves maintainability and mobile UX.

title: feat: In-game live goal tracking with scorer attribution
description:
## Summary
Enhance the live scoring experience by allowing goal-by-goal tracking with scorer names and timestamps, instead of just final scores.

## Motivation
Chega+ offers a real-time match mode with a timer and goal logging. We already have `watch/WatchScoreTracker.tsx` for basic score tracking, but enriching it with goal attribution would make `GameHistory` entries much more interesting — instead of just "3-2", you'd see "Player A 12', Player B 34', Player C 67'".

## Proposed Implementation
- Extend the watch score tracker to log individual goals:
  - Tap a team to add a goal
  - Select the scorer from the team's player list
  - Record the game minute (from a running clock)
- Store goal events as JSON in `GameHistory.teamsSnapshot` or a new `goals` JSON field:
  ```json
  [{"player": "João", "team": "Ninjas", "minute": 12}, ...]
  ```
- Display goal timeline in the history view (HistoryPage.tsx)
- Track top scorers in player stats

## Existing Code
- `src/components/watch/WatchScoreTracker.tsx` — extend this
- `src/components/HistoryPage.tsx` — display goal details

## Priority
Medium — enriches existing features, moderate effort.

title: ui: Add a proper landing page / hero section
description:
## Summary
Replace the current homepage (which goes straight to `CreateEventForm`) with a proper landing page that explains the value proposition before asking users to create a game.

## Current State
`src/pages/index.astro` renders `CreateEventForm` directly with no context. New visitors land on a form without understanding what the app does.

## Proposed Design
- Hero section with:
  - Headline: "Organize your game in 30 seconds" (localized)
  - Brief description of key features
  - Screenshot or illustration
  - Primary CTA button → scrolls to or navigates to the create form
- Feature highlights section (3-4 cards):
  - Team randomization
  - Payment splitting
  - ELO rankings
  - Push notifications
- Social proof (if available): number of games organized, players, etc.
- The create form below the fold, or on a separate `/create` route

## Benefits
- Better conversion for first-time visitors
- SEO improvement with actual content on the homepage
- Professional first impression vs. competitors

## Priority
Medium — important for growth and first impressions.

title: feat: Player reliability badge based on attendance history
description:
## Summary
Show a visible reliability indicator (green/yellow/red badge) next to player names based on their attendance rate and no-show history.

## Motivation
Chega+ surfaces player reliability as a visible badge, helping organizers know who's likely to actually show up. We already track all the data needed (attendance stats, no-show streaks in `PriorityEnrollment`) — this is purely a UI feature.

## Proposed Implementation
- Compute a reliability score from existing data:
  - Attendance rate (from `AttendanceStatsPage` data)
  - No-show streak (from `PriorityEnrollment.noShowStreak`)
  - Decline streak (from `PriorityEnrollment.declineStreak`)
- Display as a small colored dot or badge next to player names:
  - Green: >= 80% attendance, no recent no-shows
  - Yellow: 50-80% attendance or 1 recent no-show
  - Red: < 50% attendance or 2+ no-shows
- Show on:
  - Player list in `EventPage.tsx`
  - Attendance stats page
  - Optionally in the player autocomplete when adding players

## Existing Data
- `PriorityEnrollment`: `noShowStreak`, `declineStreak`
- Attendance API: `attendanceRate`, `currentStreak`
- No new schema changes needed

## Priority
Medium — low effort (UI only), helps organizers make better decisions.

title: feat: WhatsApp share with pre-formatted game message
description:
## Summary
Add a WhatsApp-optimized share button that generates a pre-formatted message with game details, making it easy to share games to WhatsApp groups.

## Motivation
WhatsApp is the primary distribution channel for amateur football games in Portugal. Competitors like FutJá use this as their main growth driver. Currently we have a generic share button, but a WhatsApp-specific flow with a rich message would significantly boost adoption.

## Proposed Implementation
- Add a `whatsapp://send?text=...` deep link button alongside the existing share button
- Pre-format the message with: game title, date/time, location, spots remaining, and the event link
- Example message:
  ```
  ⚽ Jogo na Quinta-feira 20h
  📍 Campo do Restelo
  👥 Faltam 3 lugares!
  👉 https://convocados.fly.dev/events/abc123
  ```
- Localize the message template based on the user's locale
- On desktop, fall back to `https://web.whatsapp.com/send?text=...`

## Priority
High — low effort (~1-2 hours), biggest growth impact.

## References
Inspired by FutJá's WhatsApp-first sharing approach.

title: feat: Player skill profile cards with multi-dimensional ratings
description:
## Summary
Add FIFA-style player profile cards where players are rated on multiple skill dimensions (Attack, Defense, Fitness, Teamwork, Skill) by their peers after each game.

## Motivation
Appito's most viral feature is player profile cards that look like video game cards with radar charts. This is the most shareable and differentiating feature in the amateur football app space. We already have single-number ELO ratings — multi-dimensional profiles would be much more engaging.

## Proposed Implementation

### Schema
- New `PlayerSkillRating` model:
  ```
  model PlayerSkillRating {
    id        String   @id @default(cuid())
    eventId   String
    playerName String
    raterName  String
    attack     Int     // 1-5 or 1-10
    defense    Int
    fitness    Int
    teamwork   Int
    skill      Int
    gameHistoryId String?
    createdAt  DateTime @default(now())
  }
  ```
- Aggregate ratings per player per event into an average profile

### UI
- After each game, prompt players to rate teammates on 5 attributes (quick star/slider input)
- Player profile card with radar/spider chart showing averaged attributes
- Make cards shareable (generate an OG image or downloadable card)
- Show on `PlayerStatsPage.tsx` and `RankingsPage.tsx`

### Considerations
- Rate limiting to prevent spam ratings
- Only players who participated in the game can rate
- Minimum number of ratings before showing the profile (e.g., 3 games)

## Priority
Medium-high — most differentiating feature, but requires more effort than MVP voting.

title: feat: Post-game MVP voting
description:
## Summary
Allow players to vote for the MVP (Most Valuable Player) after each game, adding a fun social element to the post-game experience.

## Motivation
FutJá and similar apps use post-game voting to drive engagement. It gives players a reason to come back to the app after the game ends and adds a competitive/social layer that makes the app stickier.

## Proposed Implementation
- After a game is recorded in history (within the editable window), show a "Vote MVP" prompt
- Each player who participated can cast one vote for another player (not themselves)
- Display the MVP badge on the game history entry
- Track total MVP awards in player stats (`PlayerStatsPage.tsx`)
- Schema options:
  - Simple: add a `mvpVotes` JSON field on `GameHistory` (e.g. `{"playerName": voteCount}`)
  - Or a small `MvpVote` model: `gameHistoryId, voterName, votedForName`

## UI
- A simple card/modal after game recording: "Who was the MVP?" with player avatars/names to tap
- Gold star/trophy badge next to MVP name in history view
- MVP count shown on player stats and rankings pages

## Priority
High — simple to implement, high engagement value.

title: chore: migrate CI workflows from npm to pnpm and optimize for cost
description:
## Context

The project uses pnpm (with `pnpm-lock.yaml`) but all 4 CI workflows still use `npm ci` with a stale `package-lock.json`. This causes:
- Dependency resolution mismatches (e.g. `send` package not properly hoisted, `express` version conflicts)
- Slower installs (npm re-resolves everything; pnpm reuses its content-addressable store)
- Wasted CI minutes and money

The Dockerfile was already migrated to pnpm in the recent production fix. The CI should follow.

## Workflows to update

All 4 files under `.github/workflows/`:

### 1. `test.yml` (CI) — 3 jobs: `ci`, `e2e`, `lighthouse`
### 2. `release.yml` — 2 jobs: `release`, `deploy`
### 3. `deploy.yml` — 1 job: `deploy`
### 4. `performance.yml` — 4 jobs: `smoke-test`, `load-test`, `stress-test`, `spike-test`

## Action items

### A. Switch all jobs from npm to pnpm

For every job across all 4 workflows:

- [ ] Replace `cache: npm` with `cache: pnpm` in `actions/setup-node`
- [ ] Add `pnpm/action-setup@v4` step before `actions/setup-node` (or use corepack)
- [ ] Replace `npm ci` with `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`
- [ ] Replace `npx <cmd>` with `pnpm <cmd>` (prisma generate, vitest, tsc, playwright, lhci)
- [ ] Replace `npm run build` with `pnpm build`
- [ ] Replace `npm pkg set` in release.yml with `node -e` or `pnpm pkg set`

### B. Optimize CI for cost/speed

- [ ] **Run ci job steps in parallel where possible**: typecheck and tests do not depend on each other — split into parallel jobs or use background processes
- [ ] **Cache Prisma engines**: Prisma downloads ~40MB of engines on every run. Cache `~/.cache/prisma` or `node_modules/.prisma`
- [ ] **Cache Playwright browsers** in e2e job: `~/.cache/ms-playwright` to avoid re-downloading ~200MB of Chromium
- [ ] **Remove redundant install+test in deploy.yml**: The deploy workflow runs on release events, which only fire after CI passes. Running tests again is redundant
- [ ] **Remove redundant install+test in release.yml deploy job**: Same issue — just deploy
- [ ] **Share build artifacts between ci, e2e, and lighthouse**: Both e2e and lighthouse re-install deps, re-generate Prisma, and re-build. Use upload/download-artifact to pass build output from ci to dependent jobs
- [ ] **Deduplicate k6 test jobs in performance.yml**: All 4 jobs have identical setup steps. Extract into a reusable workflow or use a matrix strategy

### C. Cleanup

- [ ] Delete `package-lock.json` from the repo (stale and no longer used)
- [ ] Add `package-lock.json` to `.gitignore`
- [ ] Ensure `.npmrc` with `shamefully-hoist=true` and `node-linker=hoisted` is committed

## Expected impact

- **Faster installs**: pnpm with cache is ~2-3x faster than npm ci
- **Less redundant work**: removing duplicate install+test+build steps across dependent jobs
- **Lower CI costs**: fewer minutes per workflow run, especially on PRs
- **Consistency**: CI matches local dev and Docker build (all pnpm)

title: feat: Separate active games from archived ones on /dashboard
description:

title: Show ranking button on the main page
description:
## Description

Since new players are automatically added to the ranking, the ranking button should be visible on the main page so users can easily access it.

## Current behavior

The ranking feature exists but the button to access it is not shown on the main page.
