title: perf: Replace SSE with simple polling interval
description:
## Problem

SSE (Server-Sent Events) holds a persistent HTTP connection per browser tab viewing an event. This:
- Eats Fly.io concurrency slots (hard limit: 25) — 15 viewers = 15 permanently occupied slots
- Causes a "thundering herd" on mutations: every SSE update triggers all clients to `fetchEvent()` simultaneously
- Keeps Node.js stream objects + heartbeat timers in memory per connection
- Adds server-side complexity (`sse.server.ts`, `sseManager`, 23 broadcast call sites)

For a pickup sports app where updates happen every few minutes, persistent connections are overkill.

## Comparison: useSWR vs simple polling

| | useSWR | Simple `setInterval` polling |
|---|---|---|
| New dependency | Yes (~4KB gzip) | None |
| Refactor scope | Large — every `fetchEvent()` call (~20+ sites) must become `mutate()` | Minimal — replace 1 SSE `useEffect`, remove `sseRefreshKey` |
| Features | Dedup, cache, revalidateOnFocus, refreshInterval | Just polling (focus revalidation already exists) |
| Complexity | New API to learn, SWR cache semantics | 3 lines of code |

**Recommendation: simple polling.** The app already has manual `fetchEvent()` on focus and after mutations. The only missing piece is a background refresh interval. SWR would require touching 20+ mutation call sites for marginal benefit.

## Proposal

### 1. Replace SSE with polling in `EventPage.tsx`

Replace:
```typescript
const [sseRefreshKey, setSseRefreshKey] = useState(0);

useEffect(() => {
  const es = new EventSource(`/api/events/${eventId}/stream`);
  es.addEventListener("update", () => {
    fetchEvent();
    setSseRefreshKey((k) => k + 1);
  });
  return () => es.close();
}, [eventId, fetchEvent]);
```

With:
```typescript
useEffect(() => {
  const id = setInterval(fetchEvent, 10_000); // 10s poll
  return () => clearInterval(id);
}, [fetchEvent]);
```

### 2. Remove `sseRefreshKey`

`PaymentSection` uses `refreshKey` to re-fetch costs on SSE updates. With polling, replace it with its own `setInterval` or pass the event data down directly.

### 3. Delete SSE server infrastructure

- Delete `src/pages/api/events/[id]/stream.ts`
- Delete `src/lib/sse.server.ts`
- Remove all 23 `sseManager.broadcast(...)` calls from API routes
- Remove `sseManager` imports

### 4. Optional: Add ETag support to `/api/events/[id]`

Return `ETag` header based on `updatedAt` timestamp. Clients send `If-None-Match`, server returns `304 Not Modified` with no body when nothing changed. This makes most polls nearly free.

## Impact

- **Frees all SSE concurrency slots** — every connection becomes short-lived
- **Eliminates thundering herd** — polls are staggered naturally across clients
- **Removes ~100 lines of server code** (sse.server.ts + broadcast calls)
- **Lower memory baseline** — no persistent streams or heartbeat timers

## Effort

~1-2 hours (mostly removing broadcast calls from 19 API route files)

title: perf: Prune dev dependencies from production Docker image
description:
## Problem

The production Docker image copies all of `node_modules` including dev dependencies (vitest, playwright, testing-library, etc.), increasing image size and cold start time.

## Proposal

Add a production-only install stage in the Dockerfile:

```dockerfile
FROM base AS prod-deps
COPY package.json package-lock.json ./
RUN npm ci --omit=dev
```

Then use `prod-deps` instead of `deps` in the production stage:

```dockerfile
COPY --from=prod-deps /app/node_modules ./node_modules
```

## Impact

**Medium** — smaller image, faster cold starts after suspend.

## Effort

~15 minutes

title: perf: Lazy-load heavy modules (web-push, resend, recharts)
description:
## Problem

Modules like `web-push`, `resend` (email), and `recharts` are loaded at startup even though most requests don't need them. This increases baseline memory usage and startup time.

## Proposal

Use dynamic imports in the routes that need them:

```typescript
// Instead of top-level import
const webpush = await import("web-push");
```

Apply to:
- `web-push` in push notification routes
- `resend` in email-related routes
- `recharts` (if server-rendered)

## Impact

**Low-medium** — faster startup, lower baseline memory.

## Effort

~1 hour

title: perf: Lower Fly.io concurrency limits for 256MB machine
description:
## Problem

Current `fly.toml` concurrency limits (hard: 25, soft: 20) are aggressive for a 256MB machine. Each concurrent connection holds memory for request context, Prisma queries, SSE streams, etc.

## Proposal

Lower the limits in `fly.toml`:

```toml
[http_service.concurrency]
  type = "connections"
  hard_limit = 15
  soft_limit = 10
```

This lets Fly spin up another machine under load rather than OOMing the current one.

## Impact

**Medium** — prevents memory pressure under concurrent load.

## Effort

~5 minutes

title: perf: Add SQLite mmap_size PRAGMA for better read performance
description:
## Problem

SQLite allocates its own read buffers. On a memory-constrained machine, using the OS page cache via memory-mapped I/O is more efficient.

## Proposal

Add to `applyPragmas` in `src/lib/db.server.ts`:

```typescript
await client.$queryRawUnsafe("PRAGMA mmap_size = 67108864"); // 64MB
```

This lets SQLite use the OS page cache instead of allocating its own buffers.

## Impact

**Low-medium** — better read performance, more efficient memory usage.

## Effort

~5 minutes

title: perf: Move rate limiting from SQLite to in-memory
description:
## Problem

Every API request does 1-2 SQLite queries just for rate limiting (`apiRateLimit.server.ts` and `rateLimit.server.ts`). This is unnecessary I/O on a single-instance Fly machine.

## Proposal

Replace the SQLite-backed rate limiting with an in-memory `Map` with TTL cleanup. Since we run a single Fly machine, there's no need to persist rate limits across restarts.

- Replace `checkApiRateLimit` and `checkRateLimit` to use in-memory store
- Add a `setInterval` cleanup for expired entries (every 60s)
- Remove the `RateLimit` model from `schema.prisma`
- Create a migration to drop the `RateLimit` table

## Impact

**High** — eliminates 2 DB round-trips per request.

## Effort

~1 hour

title: perf: Pre-render static pages with Astro
description:
## Problem

`astro.config.mjs` uses `output: "server"` for everything. Static pages (docs, landing, etc.) are server-rendered on every request, wasting CPU and memory.

## Proposal

Add `export const prerender = true;` to pages that don't need dynamic data:

- Landing/home page
- Docs pages
- Any other static content

These will be served as static files with zero Node.js processing per request.

## Impact

**Medium** — zero-cost serving for static routes, less memory allocation.

## Effort

~30 minutes (audit pages and add prerender flag)

title: perf: Add Node.js memory flags for 256MB Fly machine
description:
## Problem

Node.js defaults to a large heap size and lazy GC, which can cause OOM on a 256MB Fly machine.

## Proposal

Update `scripts/start.sh` to pass memory flags:

```sh
exec node --max-old-space-size=200 --max-semi-space-size=2 dist/server/entry.mjs
```

This tells V8 to GC more aggressively within the 256MB constraint.

## Impact

**Medium** — prevents OOM kills under load.

## Effort

~5 minutes

title: feat: PWA score tracker for smartwatches
description:
## Summary

Minimal PWA for tracking game scores on Apple Watch (watchOS 10+) and Wear OS. No background timer — just score tracking with offline support and sync.

## Motivation

Players want to quickly update scores from their wrist during a game without pulling out their phone. A PWA avoids the cost of building native watch apps while covering the core use case.

## Scope

### In scope
- New `/watch` route with minimal UI optimized for small screens (~200px)
- Score increment/decrement per team with large tap targets (≥44pt)
- Offline support via service worker + IndexedDB for storing pending score updates
- Background sync to `POST /api/events/{eventId}/games` when connectivity returns
- "Add to Home Screen" support (PWA manifest)
- Game select screen showing today's events
- **PWA subpath support**: The watch PWA must work correctly under the `/watch` subpath, with its own scoped manifest and service worker

### Out of scope
- Background timer (not reliable in watch browsers — OS suspends the tab)
- Haptic feedback, always-on display, complications
- Native watch app
- Changing the main app's `base` config — the watch PWA is a separate scoped PWA

## UX Flow (2 screens)

```
┌─────────────┐    ┌──────────────────┐
│  Today's    │    │                  │
│  Games      │───►│  Team A  2       │
│             │    │  [−]       [+]   │
│  ▸ Futsal   │    │                  │
│  ▸ Basket   │    │  Team B  1       │
└─────────────┘    │  [−]       [+]   │
                   │                  │
                   │  [Submit]        │
                   └──────────────────┘
```

## Technical Notes

- Build within existing Astro/React stack
- Minimal JS bundle — keep it as light as possible for watch browsers
- Store pending syncs in IndexedDB, flush on connectivity
- High contrast, no text input, everything is taps
- Test on Apple Watch simulator (watchOS 10+ Safari) and Wear OS emulator

### PWA Subpath Architecture

The watch PWA runs under `/watch` as a **separate scoped PWA** alongside the main app:

1. **Separate manifest** at `/watch/manifest.json` with:
   - `"start_url": "/watch/"`
   - `"scope": "/watch/"`
2. **Separate service worker** at `/watch/sw.js` scoped to `/watch/`
3. **No changes to main app** — the root `/manifest.json`, `/sw.js`, and `astro.config.mjs` remain untouched
4. The `/watch` pages link to `/watch/manifest.json` instead of `/manifest.json`

This approach allows both PWAs (main app + watch tracker) to coexist independently.

## API

May need a lightweight sync endpoint:

```
POST /api/events/:eventId/watch-sync
Body: { scores: [number, number] }
```

Or reuse existing game/score update endpoints if they fit.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `/watch` route renders on a 198×242px viewport (Apple Watch SE)
- [ ] Scores can be incremented/decremented per team
- [ ] Works offline after first load
- [ ] Syncs scores to server when back online
- [ ] PWA installable on watch (manifest + service worker scoped to `/watch`)
- [ ] Main app PWA remains unaffected
- [ ] `/watch/manifest.json` has correct `start_url` and `scope`
- [ ] `/watch/sw.js` only controls `/watch/*` routes

title: Increase test coverage threshold to 95%
description:
## Goal

Increase the branch coverage threshold from 84% to 95%.

## Context

The current CI threshold is 84% for branch coverage. During PR #170 we hit the threshold limit when adding new endpoints without full branch coverage. Raising the bar to 95% will catch more edge cases and prevent regressions.

## Tasks

- [ ] Audit current coverage report to identify files with lowest branch coverage
- [ ] Add tests for uncovered branches in API endpoints (especially `stream.ts`, `calendar.ics.ts`, `status.ts`, `randomize.ts`, `players.ts`)
- [ ] Add tests for uncovered branches in utility/server libraries
- [ ] Incrementally raise the threshold (84% → 88% → 92% → 95%)
- [ ] Update `vitest.config.ts` coverage thresholds at each step

title: feat: Admin dashboard UI with aggregated metrics and env-based admin config
description:
## Summary

Build a UI dashboard at `/admin` that displays aggregated metrics to measure the success of the app. The instance admin should be configured via an `ADMIN_EMAIL` environment variable (defaults to none — no admin).

## Requirements

### Admin identification via env var
- Replace the current `role` field-based admin check with an `ADMIN_EMAIL` env var
- `ADMIN_EMAIL` defaults to empty (no admin by default)
- `isAdmin()` should compare the authenticated user's email against `ADMIN_EMAIL`
- Remove the `role` column from the User model (migration)

### Enhanced stats API
Expand `GET /api/admin/stats` to return aggregated success metrics:
- Total users, total events
- Total games played (from GameHistory)
- Active events (events with dateTime in the future)
- Active users (users who joined a game in the last 30 days)
- Games played in the last 7 and 30 days
- Average players per event
- Recurring vs one-off event breakdown
- Sport distribution (count per sport type)

### Admin dashboard UI (`/admin`)
- New Astro page + React component following existing patterns (ThemeModeProvider + ResponsiveLayout)
- Summary stat cards (total users, events, games played, active users)
- Sport distribution breakdown
- Recurring vs one-off split
- Recent activity metrics (7d / 30d)
- User list with search and pagination (reuse existing `/api/admin/users`)
- Protected: only accessible to the admin user

### i18n
- Add all new strings to all 6 locale files (en, pt, es, fr, de, it)

### Tests
- Unit tests for updated `isAdmin()` with env var
- Unit tests for expanded `getAdminStats()`
- Tests verifying non-admin users get 403

Closes #68 (extends the admin backend from PR #90 with a UI)

title: fix: Random game titles only use correct language for English and Portuguese
description:
## Bug

Random game titles generated in `CreateEventForm` only work correctly for English and Portuguese. All other locales (es, fr, de, it) fall back to English titles despite having full translations available in `randomTitles.ts`.

## Root Cause

In `src/components/CreateEventForm.tsx` (lines 48 and 137), the locale is hardcoded to only recognize Portuguese:

```typescript
getRandomTitle(locale === "pt" ? "pt" : "en")
```

This means Spanish, French, German, and Italian users always get English titles, even though `src/lib/randomTitles.ts` already contains translated titles for all 6 locales.

## Fix

Replace the ternary with a direct locale pass-through:

```typescript
getRandomTitle(locale as TitleLocale)
```

The `getRandomTitle` function already handles unknown locales by falling back to English (`titles[locale] ?? titles.en`), so no additional safety is needed.

### Files to change
- `src/components/CreateEventForm.tsx` — line 48 (initial state) and line 137 (re-randomize button)

## Additional: Improve test coverage

The existing `randomTitles.test.ts` tests only check that each locale returns a non-empty string. They do not validate that the returned titles are actually in the correct language. The `i18n.test.ts` should also be extended.

### Test improvements needed:
1. **`src/test/randomTitles.test.ts`** — For each locale, verify that the returned title is from that locale's pool (not from the English pool). For example:
   - `getRandomTitle("es")` should return a title that exists in the Spanish titles array, not the English one
   - `getRandomTitle("fr")` should return a title from the French pool
   - etc.

2. **`src/test/i18n.test.ts`** — Add a test that validates every supported locale has its own random titles pool in `randomTitles.ts` and that none of them are identical to the English pool (which would indicate missing translations).

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `getRandomTitle` receives the actual detected locale (not hardcoded en/pt)
- [ ] Spanish, French, German, and Italian users see titles in their language
- [ ] Tests validate that each locale returns titles from its own language pool
- [ ] i18n tests verify all locales have distinct random title pools

title: feat: Allow event owners to share admin rights with other users
description:
## Summary

Event owners should be able to delegate admin rights to other users so that events can be managed even when the owner is unavailable.

## Problem

Currently, only the event owner (`Event.ownerId`) can manage an event. If the owner is busy or unavailable, nobody else can make changes (add/remove players, update details, manage costs, etc.).

## Proposed Solution

### New `EventAdmin` model

Add a join table linking events to users with an admin role:

```prisma
model EventAdmin {
  id        String   @id @default(cuid())
  eventId   String
  event     Event    @relation(fields: [eventId], references: [id], onDelete: Cascade)
  userId    String
  user      User     @relation("EventAdmins", fields: [userId], references: [id], onDelete: Cascade)
  createdAt DateTime @default(now())

  @@unique([eventId, userId])
  @@index([userId])
}
```

Update `Event` and `User` models with the new relation.

### API Endpoints

- **`POST /api/events/[eventId]/admins`** — Add an admin by searching for their email
- **`DELETE /api/events/[eventId]/admins/[userId]`** — Remove an admin
- **`GET /api/events/[eventId]/admins`** — List current admins for an event

Only the event **owner** should be able to add/remove admins.

### Authorization Changes

All existing ownership checks (e.g. `event.ownerId === user.id`) need to also accept event admins. Create a shared helper like:

```typescript
function canManageEvent(event: Event, userId: string, eventAdmins: EventAdmin[]): boolean {
  return event.ownerId === userId || eventAdmins.some(a => a.userId === userId);
}
```

### UI Changes

- Add an "Admins" section in the event settings page
- Email search input to find users and grant admin rights
- List of current admins with a remove button
- i18n strings for all 6 locales (en, pt, es, fr, de, it)

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Owner can search for a user by email and grant them admin rights
- [ ] Owner can revoke admin rights from a user
- [ ] Admins can perform the same management actions as the owner (except adding/removing other admins)
- [ ] Non-owners/non-admins cannot manage the event
- [ ] Rate limiting applied to admin mutation endpoints
- [ ] Tests cover adding, removing, and authorization logic (TDD)
- [ ] i18n strings added for all 6 locales

## Labels

enhancement

title: feat: Structured payment methods with deep links (MBWay, Revolut, Phone)
description:
## Summary

Replace the current free-text `paymentDetails` field on `EventCost` with structured payment method types. Each method should use deep links / universal links to open the corresponding app directly — ideally pre-filled with the payment amount and recipient.

## Current State

- `EventCost.paymentDetails` is a free-text `String?` (e.g. `"Revolut @jose / MB Way 912345678"`)
- `PlayerPayment.method` is also free-text `String?` (e.g. `"revolut"`, `"cash"`)
- No deep linking or app integration exists

## Proposed Payment Method Types

### 1. Phone Number (SMS/Call)
- Store: phone number (with country code)
- Deep link: `tel:+351912345678` or `sms:+351912345678`
- UI: phone input with country code selector

### 2. MB Way
- Store: phone number associated with MB Way account
- Deep link: `mbway://` scheme (if available), otherwise fall back to displaying the number for manual entry
- Research needed: MB Way does not have a well-documented public deep link scheme for pre-filled payments. The most reliable approach may be displaying the phone number with a "copy" button and instructions.
- UI: phone number input, display with copy-to-clipboard

### 3. Revolut
- Two sub-types to support:

#### 3a. Revolut Tag (`@username`)
- Store: Revolut username (e.g. `jose`)
- Deep link: `https://revolut.me/<username>` — this is a universal link that opens the Revolut app if installed, or the web payment page otherwise
- Bonus: append amount query param if supported (e.g. `https://revolut.me/<username>?amount=20&currency=EUR`)
- UI: text input with `@` prefix

#### 3b. Revolut Payment Link
- Store: full `revolut.me` URL or `rev.money` short link
- Deep link: the URL itself is already a universal link
- UI: URL input with validation for `revolut.me/*` or `rev.money/*` domains

## Data Model Changes

Add a new model or structured fields to `EventCost`:

```
Option A — JSON field (simpler, SQLite-friendly):
  paymentMethods  String?  // JSON: [{ type: "mbway", value: "912345678" }, { type: "revolut_tag", value: "jose" }, ...]

Option B — Separate table:
  model EventPaymentMethod {
    id          String    @id @default(cuid())
    eventCostId String
    eventCost   EventCost @relation(...)
    type        String    // "phone" | "mbway" | "revolut_tag" | "revolut_link"
    value       String    // phone number, username, or URL
    label       String?   // optional display label
  }
```

Keep `paymentDetails` as a deprecated/fallback free-text field during migration, or migrate existing data.

## UI Changes

### Event Owner (Edit Mode)
- Replace the free-text `paymentDetails` textarea with a structured form
- "Add payment method" button with type selector (Phone, MB Way, Revolut Tag, Revolut Link)
- Each method shows an appropriate input (phone input, username input, URL input)
- Allow multiple methods per event

### Player View (Read Mode)
- Display each payment method as a tappable/clickable chip or button
- Tapping opens the deep link:
  - Phone → `tel:` link
  - MB Way → copy number + instructions (or deep link if scheme is confirmed)
  - Revolut Tag → opens `https://revolut.me/<username>` (optionally with amount)
  - Revolut Link → opens the provided URL directly
- Keep the copy-to-clipboard fallback for all methods

## Deep Link Reference

| Type | Deep Link | Fallback |
|------|-----------|----------|
| Phone | `tel:+<number>` | Copy number |
| MB Way | `mbway://` (TBD) | Copy number + instructions |
| Revolut Tag | `https://revolut.me/<user>?amount=X&currency=Y` | Copy tag |
| Revolut Link | Direct URL (`revolut.me/*` or `rev.money/*`) | Copy link |

## Tasks

- [ ] Research MB Way deep link scheme availability
- [ ] Design data model (JSON field vs separate table)
- [ ] Add migration for new payment method storage
- [ ] Build structured payment method form component
- [ ] Implement deep link generation per method type
- [ ] Update `PaymentSection.tsx` read-mode to show clickable method buttons
- [ ] Update API routes (`cost` PUT) to accept structured methods
- [ ] Migrate existing free-text `paymentDetails` data (best-effort parsing)
- [ ] Add i18n strings for all 6 locales
- [ ] Write tests for deep link generation and API changes
- [ ] Update `GameHistory` payments snapshot to include structured methods

## Notes

- Revolut `revolut.me` links work as universal links — they open the app on mobile or the web payment page on desktop. This is the most reliable integration.
- MB Way deep linking is less standardized. We may need to settle for "copy number" UX with clear instructions.
- Consider adding a "custom/other" type to preserve flexibility for methods we do not explicitly support yet.

title: roadmap: Implementation priority order for open issues
description:
## Implementation Priority Order

Prioritized based on: dependency chains, user impact, effort, and infrastructure needs.

---

### Tier 1 — Infrastructure & Compliance (do first)
Critical foundations that other features depend on or that are legally required.

| Priority | Issue | Rationale |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **1** | #133 Configure Litestream backups with Cloudflare R2 | **Data safety first.** No backup = one bad deploy away from losing everything. Pure ops work, no code changes, 30 min. |
| **2** | #107 Account deletion (right to erasure) | **GDPR legal requirement.** Users can already create accounts — they must be able to delete them. Small scope. |
| **3** | #108 User data export (GDPR compliance) | **GDPR legal requirement.** Right to data portability. Can reuse the same data gathering logic from #107. |
| **4** | #106 Password change | **Basic auth hygiene.** Users expect this. Small scope, better-auth likely has built-in support. |
| **5** | #44 Magic link login | **Reduces friction.** Many casual players won't create passwords. better-auth has a `magicLink` plugin. Pairs well with #136 (priority players need accounts). |

### Tier 2 — Core UX Improvements (high impact, moderate effort)
Features that directly improve the daily experience for existing users.

| Priority | Issue | Rationale |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **6** | #135 PWA install prompt + update banner | **Low effort, high visibility.** The PWA infra is already there — just needs the install prompt UI. Improves retention. |
| **7** | #112 Notification settings + email enablement | **Unblocks email notifications.** Email functions exist but aren't wired up. #79 is a subset of this. Needed before #136 (priority enrollment sends emails). |
| **8** | #79 Notification management | **Covered by #112.** Close as duplicate or merge into #112 when implementing. |
| **9** | #134 Player attendance statistics | **Prerequisite for #136.** Surfaces data that already exists. Moderate effort (calculation logic + UI + API). |
| **10** | #122 Event activity log | **Transparency.** Helps organizers understand what happened. Moderate effort but standalone — no dependencies. |

### Tier 3 — Differentiating Features (what makes the app special)
Features that set Convocados apart and drive engagement.

| Priority | Issue | Rationale |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **11** | #136 Priority enrollment (Auto-Convocação) | **The big feature.** Depends on #134 (attendance) and #112 (email notifications). High effort but high user value — this is what the user specifically asked for. |
| **12** | #117 Event password/access control | **Privacy.** Some games are private. Moderate effort. Standalone. |
| **13** | #51 Game comments/chat | **Social glue.** Simple comment thread per event. Low-moderate effort, high engagement value. |
| **14** | #52 Friends/Groups | **Stickiness.** Persistent player groups that auto-populate games. Moderate effort. Reduces friction for recurring organizers. |
| **15** | #53 Player stats dashboard (cross-game) | **Engagement.** Builds on #134 attendance data + existing ELO/ratings. Fun but not critical. |

### Tier 4 — Scale & Platform (future)
Nice to have, do when the core is solid.

| Priority | Issue | Rationale |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **16** | #105 k6 load testing | **Insurance.** Important before scaling, but current traffic doesn't demand it yet. |
| **17** | #60 Embeddable widget | **Growth channel.** Venue/booking platform integration. High effort, speculative value. |
| **18** | #70 Native mobile app | **Largest effort.** PWA (#135) covers 90% of the mobile need. Defer until PWA limitations are proven. |

---

## Dependency Graph

```
#133 Litestream R2 (standalone)
#107 Account deletion (standalone)
#108 Data export (standalone, shares logic with #107)
#106 Password change (standalone)
#44  Magic link (standalone)
#135 PWA install (standalone)
#112 Notifications ──┐
#79  (subset of #112) │
#134 Attendance ──────┼──→ #136 Priority Enrollment
                      │         │
                      │         └──→ #53 Cross-game stats
#122 Activity log (standalone)
#117 Event access control (standalone)
#51  Comments (standalone)
#52  Groups (standalone)
#105 Load testing (standalone)
#60  Widget (standalone)
#70  Native app (standalone, after #135)
```

## Suggested Sprints

**Sprint 1 (infra + compliance):** #133, #107, #108, #106
**Sprint 2 (auth + UX):** #44, #135, #112/#79
**Sprint 3 (data + transparency):** #134, #122
**Sprint 4 (the big feature):** #136
**Sprint 5 (social):** #117, #51, #52, #53
**Sprint 6 (scale):** #105, #60, #70

title: feat: Priority enrollment for consistent players (Auto-Convocação)
description:
## Problem

In recurring pickup games, reliable players who show up every week have to race to sign up each time, competing equally with flaky players who bail half the time. This feels unfair and creates unnecessary anxiety. Organizers also waste time chasing confirmations from unreliable players while dependable ones miss out on spots.

A user requested a way to "compensate consistent players" by automatically reserving their spot and only requiring confirmation.

## Proposal: Priority Enrollment (Auto-Convocação)

An opt-in system where the event owner enables priority enrollment for recurring games. Eligible players are automatically added to the next game instance and asked to confirm. Unconfirmed spots are released before the game.

### Depends On
- **#134 Player attendance statistics** — required to calculate eligibility. Must be implemented first.

---

## Design

### Eligibility: Earned, Not Just Appointed

To keep things fair and transparent, eligibility is primarily attendance-based with owner override:

1. **Auto-qualification**: Players who attended at least `threshold` of the last `window` games qualify automatically (e.g. 3 of last 4). The criteria are visible to everyone.
2. **Owner override**: The owner can manually add or remove players from the priority list (injuries, new regulars, etc.).
3. **Player opt-in**: Priority players must have a linked account and must opt in. Some people prefer to decide week by week.

### The Flow

```
Recurring game resets (1h after game ends, per existing logic)
  │
  ├─ System identifies priority-eligible players:
  │   ├─ Attendance threshold met? ✓
  │   ├─ Player has linked account? ✓
  │   └─ Player opted in to auto-enrollment? ✓
  │
  ├─ Auto-enroll eligible players (up to priority cap)
  │   └─ Player status: "pending_confirmation"
  │
  ├─ Send notifications (email via Resend + push)
  │   "You're in for Tuesday 5-a-side! Confirm by [deadline] or your spot opens up."
  │   Includes: one-tap confirm link, one-tap decline link
  │
  ├─ Confirmation window (configurable, default: closes 48h before game)
  │   ├─ Player confirms → status becomes normal "playing"
  │   ├─ Player declines → spot released immediately
  │   └─ No response by deadline → spot released, player notified
  │
  └─ Remaining spots open for manual sign-up (existing flow, unchanged)
```

### Fairness Rules

| Rule | Rationale |
|------|-----------|
| **Spot cap**: Priority reserves at most 60-70% of `maxPlayers` (configurable) | Always leave room for new/casual players — prevents the group from becoming a closed club |
| **Minimum games**: Require at least `minGames` played before attendance rate kicks in (e.g. 3) | Don't penalize newcomers who just joined |
| **Transparency**: Show threshold, window, and each player's current attendance rate on the event page | No hidden criteria — everyone knows the rules |
| **Decline is free**: Declining a priority spot has no penalty | Honest communication should be encouraged |
| **No-show penalty**: Confirming but not showing up counts as a negative signal — lose priority after 2 consecutive no-shows | Wasting a confirmed spot is worse than declining |
| **Decay**: If a priority player declines 3 times in a row, auto-enrollment pauses until they manually join a game | Prevents zombie reservations |

### Priority Order (When More Eligible Than Spots)

If more players qualify than the priority cap allows:
1. Highest attendance rate in the window
2. Longest active streak
3. Earliest account creation (tiebreaker)

---

## Data Model

### New Models

```prisma
model PriorityEnrollment {
  id        String   @id @default(cuid())
  eventId   String
  event     Event    @relation(fields: [eventId], references: [id], onDelete: Cascade)
  userId    String
  user      User     @relation(fields: [userId], references: [id], onDelete: Cascade)
  source    String   @default("auto")  // "auto" (earned) | "manual" (owner added)
  optedIn   Boolean  @default(true)    // player can opt out
  createdAt DateTime @default(now())
  updatedAt DateTime @updatedAt

  @@unique([eventId, userId])
  @@index([eventId])
}

model PriorityConfirmation {
  id          String    @id @default(cuid())
  eventId     String
  event       Event     @relation(fields: [eventId], references: [id], onDelete: Cascade)
  userId      String
  user        User      @relation(fields: [userId], references: [id], onDelete: Cascade)
  gameDate    DateTime
  status      String    @default("pending")  // "pending" | "confirmed" | "declined" | "expired"
  notifiedAt  DateTime
  respondedAt DateTime?
  deadline    DateTime
  createdAt   DateTime  @default(now())

  @@unique([eventId, userId, gameDate])
  @@index([eventId, gameDate])
  @@index([deadline])
}
```

### New Fields on Event

```prisma
// Priority enrollment settings
priorityEnabled       Boolean @default(false)
priorityThreshold     Int     @default(3)   // min games attended in window
priorityWindow        Int     @default(4)   // last N games to evaluate
priorityMaxPercent    Int     @default(70)  // max % of maxPlayers reserved for priority
priorityDeadlineHours Int     @default(48)  // hours before game to close confirmations
priorityMinGames      Int     @default(3)   // min total games before eligible
```

---

## API Endpoints

### Settings
- `PUT /api/events/:id/priority` — enable/disable and configure priority settings (owner only)

### Enrollment Management
- `GET /api/events/:id/priority` — list priority-enrolled players with their status and stats
- `POST /api/events/:id/priority/:userId` — owner manually adds a player
- `DELETE /api/events/:id/priority/:userId` — owner removes a player
- `PUT /api/events/:id/priority/opt-out` — authenticated player opts out of auto-enrollment
- `PUT /api/events/:id/priority/opt-in` — authenticated player opts back in

### Confirmation
- `POST /api/events/:id/priority/confirm` — player confirms their spot (authenticated, or via token in email link)
- `POST /api/events/:id/priority/decline` — player declines their spot

---

## UI Components

### Owner: Priority Settings Panel
- Toggle to enable/disable
- Sliders/inputs for threshold, window, max %, deadline hours
- Preview: "With current settings, X players would qualify"

### Owner: Priority Player List
- Table showing: player name, attendance rate, source (auto/manual), opt-in status
- Actions: add manually, remove
- Visual: green badge for auto-qualified, blue for manually added

### Player: Priority Status
- On event page, show authenticated player their priority status:
  - "You're a priority player — you'll be auto-enrolled for the next game"
  - "Confirm your spot for [date]" with confirm/decline buttons
  - "You need X more games to qualify for priority"
- Opt-in/opt-out toggle in their profile or event page

### Event Page: Pending Confirmations
- Show pending priority players distinctly from confirmed players
- Countdown to confirmation deadline
- After deadline: unconfirmed spots visually released

---

## Notification Templates

### Auto-Enrollment Notification
**Subject**: "You're in for {title} on {date}!"
**Body**: "Your spot is reserved. Confirm by {deadline} or it opens up for others. [Confirm] [Decline]"

### Deadline Reminder (24h before deadline)
**Subject**: "Confirm your spot for {title} — deadline tomorrow"
**Body**: "You haven't confirmed yet. Your spot will be released on {deadline}. [Confirm] [Decline]"

### Spot Released
**Subject**: "Your spot for {title} was released"
**Body**: "The confirmation deadline passed. You can still join manually if spots are available."

---

## Implementation Order

1. **#134 Attendance stats** (prerequisite)
2. **Schema + migration**: Add priority fields to Event, create PriorityEnrollment and PriorityConfirmation tables
3. **Priority settings API + UI**: Owner can enable and configure
4. **Eligibility calculation**: Logic to determine who qualifies based on attendance
5. **Auto-enrollment on recurrence reset**: Modify the existing reset logic to auto-add priority players
6. **Confirmation flow**: API endpoints + email/push notifications + confirm/decline UI
7. **Deadline enforcement**: Cron job or check on the existing reminders cron to expire unconfirmed spots
8. **Priority status display**: Show priority info on event page for both owners and players

## Tests

- Unit tests for eligibility calculation (threshold, window, min games, decay)
- Unit tests for priority ordering when more eligible than spots
- API tests for all endpoints (settings, enrollment, confirmation)
- Integration test for the full flow: reset → auto-enroll → notify → confirm → play
- Edge cases: owner disables mid-cycle, player unlinks account, game cancelled

title: feat: PWA install prompt and improved update banner
description:
## Problem

The app is already a valid PWA (manifest.json, service worker, icons), but there is no install prompt to encourage users to add it to their home screen. The browser's native "Add to Home Screen" prompt is easy to miss. Additionally, the existing `UpdateBanner` component works but could be more polished.

## Inspiration

Convocados.pt shows a dedicated install banner at the bottom of the page with the app icon, a description, and Install/Dismiss buttons. This is a common PWA pattern that significantly increases install rates.

## Current State

Already in place:
- `public/manifest.json` — valid PWA manifest with icons (192, 512, maskable)
- `public/sw.js` — service worker with push notifications
- `ResponsiveLayout.tsx` → `UpdateBanner` component — detects new SW version and shows "A new version is available" slide-down banner
- i18n keys: `updateAvailable`, `updateNow`

Missing:
- No `beforeinstallprompt` event handling
- No install banner/prompt UI
- No tracking of whether user dismissed or installed
- Update banner could show what changed (version number)

## Requirements

### 1. Install Prompt Banner
- Listen for the `beforeinstallprompt` event in `ResponsiveLayout`
- Show a dismissible banner when the app is installable (not already installed)
- Banner should include:
  - App icon (from manifest)
  - Short message: "Install Convocados for quick access — no app store needed"
  - "Install" button (triggers the native install prompt)
  - "Not now" dismiss button
- Persist dismissal in `localStorage` with a cooldown (e.g. don't show again for 7 days)
- Don't show if app is already running in standalone mode (`window.matchMedia("(display-mode: standalone)").matches`)

### 2. Install Detection
- Detect successful install via `appinstalled` event
- Hide the banner immediately on install
- Optionally track install in localStorage for analytics

### 3. Improved Update Banner
The existing `UpdateBanner` already works well. Minor improvements:
- Show the version number in the banner (use `__APP_VERSION__` global)
- e.g. "Version 3.12.0 available" instead of just "A new version is available"
- Keep the existing "Update" button behavior (postMessage SKIP_WAITING → reload)

### 4. iOS Support
- iOS Safari does not fire `beforeinstallprompt`
- For iOS, show a manual instruction banner: "Tap the share button, then 'Add to Home Screen'"
- Detect iOS via `navigator.userAgent` + check not standalone
- Same 7-day dismissal cooldown

### 5. i18n
Add keys to all 6 locales:
- `installApp` — "Install Convocados"
- `installAppDesc` — "Add to your home screen for quick access — no app store needed"
- `installBtn` — "Install"
- `installDismiss` — "Not now"
- `installIosHint` — "Tap {shareIcon} then 'Add to Home Screen'"
- `versionAvailable` — "Version {version} available"

### 6. Styling
- Use MUI `Paper` + `Slide` animation (consistent with existing `UpdateBanner`)
- Position: bottom of screen for install prompt, top for update banner (current behavior)
- Respect dark/light theme
- Mobile-first: full-width on small screens, centered card on desktop

### 7. Tests
- Component test for install banner rendering
- Test dismissal persistence (localStorage mock)
- Test standalone mode detection (should not show banner)
- Test iOS detection path

## Technical Notes
- The `beforeinstallprompt` event must be captured and stored — it can only be used once
- The event is only fired when PWA criteria are met (HTTPS, manifest, service worker)
- `event.prompt()` shows the native browser install dialog
- `event.userChoice` returns the user's decision

title: feat: Player attendance statistics dashboard
description:
## Problem

Organizers have no visibility into player reliability. When planning games, they don't know who consistently shows up vs who frequently bails. The data already exists in `GameHistory` and `Player` tables but is not surfaced anywhere.

## Inspiration

Convocados.pt shows attendance rates per player (e.g. "94% presence") in their club dashboard. For casual pickup games, a lighter version of this would help organizers know who to count on.

## Requirements

### 1. Per-Player Attendance Stats
Calculate from `GameHistory` + `teamsSnapshot` data:
- **Attendance rate**: games attended / total games (as percentage)
- **Total games played**
- **Current streak**: consecutive games attended
- **Last played**: date of most recent game

### 2. Event-Level Stats View
Add an "Attendance" tab or section to the event page (alongside History, Ratings):
- Table of all players who have ever participated, sorted by attendance rate
- Visual indicator: green (>80%), yellow (50-80%), red (<50%)
- Filter: last N games, last month, all time

### 3. API Endpoint
- `GET /api/events/:id/attendance` — returns attendance stats for all players in an event
- Response shape:
  ```json
  {
    "players": [
      {
        "name": "João",
        "gamesPlayed": 15,
        "totalGames": 20,
        "attendanceRate": 0.75,
        "currentStreak": 3,
        "lastPlayed": "2026-03-15T18:00:00Z"
      }
    ],
    "totalGames": 20
  }
  ```

### 4. Data Source
- Parse `teamsSnapshot` JSON from `GameHistory` entries with `status: "played"`
- A player "attended" a game if their name appears in either team's snapshot
- Only count games with `status: "played"` (not cancelled)

### 5. UI Component
- New `AttendanceStats` React component
- Reuse existing MUI table patterns from the codebase
- Add i18n keys for all 6 locales
- Mobile-friendly layout

### 6. Tests
- Unit tests for attendance calculation logic
- API route tests
- Component tests

## Out of Scope
- Notifications about attendance trends
- Attendance predictions
- Mandatory attendance requirements

title: feat: Configure Litestream backups with Cloudflare R2
description:
## Overview

Configure Litestream to replicate the production SQLite database to Cloudflare R2 (S3-compatible) and document the disaster recovery process.

## Background

The Litestream infrastructure is already in place (#123):
- `litestream.yml` — config with S3-compatible env var placeholders
- `scripts/start.sh` — restore-on-boot + replicate-on-run logic
- `Dockerfile` — Litestream binary installed in production image

What is missing: the actual R2 bucket setup, Fly secrets, and recovery documentation.

## Requirements

### 1. Cloudflare R2 Setup
- Create an R2 bucket (e.g. `convocados-backups`) in the Cloudflare dashboard
- Create an R2 API token with read/write access to the bucket
- Note: R2 is S3-compatible, so Litestream works with it out of the box using the S3 replica type

### 2. Update `litestream.yml` for R2
The current config should work as-is since R2 is S3-compatible, but verify:
- `endpoint` should be `https://<ACCOUNT_ID>.r2.cloudflarestorage.com`
- `region` should be `auto` (R2 does not use regions, but the S3 client requires one)
- `force-path-style` may need to be set to `true` for R2 compatibility

Example config:
```yaml
dbs:
  - path: /data/prod.db
    replicas:
      - type: s3
        bucket: ${LITESTREAM_REPLICA_BUCKET}
        path: convocados/db
        endpoint: ${LITESTREAM_REPLICA_ENDPOINT}
        region: auto
        force-path-style: true
        access-key-id: ${LITESTREAM_ACCESS_KEY_ID}
        secret-access-key: ${LITESTREAM_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY}
        retention: 168h  # 7 days
        sync-interval: 1s
```

### 3. Set Fly.io Secrets
```bash
fly secrets set \
  LITESTREAM_REPLICA_BUCKET=convocados-backups \
  LITESTREAM_REPLICA_ENDPOINT=https://<ACCOUNT_ID>.r2.cloudflarestorage.com \
  LITESTREAM_REPLICA_REGION=auto \
  LITESTREAM_ACCESS_KEY_ID=<r2-access-key-id> \
  LITESTREAM_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=<r2-secret-access-key>
```

### 4. Verify DATABASE_URL
Ensure the production `DATABASE_URL` points to the mounted volume path:
```bash
fly secrets set DATABASE_URL="file:/data/prod.db"
```

### 5. Disaster Recovery Documentation
Add a `docs/disaster-recovery.md` guide covering:

#### Automatic Recovery (already implemented)
- On boot, `scripts/start.sh` checks if `/data/prod.db` exists
- If missing, it runs `litestream restore -if-replica-exists` to pull the latest snapshot from R2
- Prisma migrations run after restore to apply any pending schema changes
- App starts with Litestream replicating in the background

#### Manual Recovery Steps
1. **SSH into the Fly machine:**
   ```bash
   fly ssh console
   ```
2. **Stop the app (if running):**
   ```bash
   kill $(pgrep -f "node dist/server/entry.mjs")
   ```
3. **Restore from R2:**
   ```bash
   litestream restore -config /app/litestream.yml /data/prod.db
   ```
4. **Restore to a specific point in time:**
   ```bash
   litestream restore -config /app/litestream.yml -timestamp "2026-03-20T12:00:00Z" /data/prod.db
   ```
5. **Verify the restored database:**
   ```bash
   sqlite3 /data/prod.db "PRAGMA integrity_check;"
   sqlite3 /data/prod.db "SELECT count(*) FROM Event;"
   ```
6. **Restart the app:**
   ```bash
   fly machines restart
   ```

#### Local Recovery (download backup locally)
1. **Install Litestream locally:**
   ```bash
   brew install litestream  # macOS
   ```
2. **Create a local litestream config** (`litestream-local.yml`):
   ```yaml
   dbs:
     - path: ./restored.db
       replicas:
         - type: s3
           bucket: convocados-backups
           endpoint: https://<ACCOUNT_ID>.r2.cloudflarestorage.com
           region: auto
           force-path-style: true
           access-key-id: <key>
           secret-access-key: <secret>
   ```
3. **Restore:**
   ```bash
   litestream restore -config litestream-local.yml ./restored.db
   ```
4. **Inspect with Prisma Studio:**
   ```bash
   DATABASE_URL="file:./restored.db" npx prisma studio
   ```

#### Listing Available Snapshots
```bash
fly ssh console -C "litestream snapshots -config /app/litestream.yml /data/prod.db"
```

#### Monitoring
- Check replication is active: `fly logs | grep litestream`
- Health endpoint reports WAL mode: `curl https://convocados.fly.dev/api/health`

### 6. Verification Checklist
After setup, verify:
- [ ] `fly deploy` succeeds
- [ ] Logs show `Starting app with Litestream replication...`
- [ ] `fly ssh console -C "litestream snapshots -config /app/litestream.yml /data/prod.db"` shows snapshots
- [ ] Destroy and recreate the machine — DB restores automatically from R2
- [ ] Health endpoint returns `journalMode: "wal"`

title: feat: Persistent rate limiting backed by SQLite
description:
## Problem

Both rate limiters (`rateLimit.server.ts` and `apiRateLimit.server.ts`) use in-memory `Map` objects. This has several problems:

1. **State lost on deploy** — every deployment resets all rate limits, allowing burst abuse immediately after
2. **State lost on machine suspend** — `fly.toml` has `auto_stop_machines = "suspend"`, so rate limits reset whenever the machine suspends and resumes (which can happen frequently with low traffic)
3. **Memory leak risk** — `apiRateLimit.server.ts` has a `setInterval` cleanup every 5 minutes, but this interval is never cleared and will leak if the module is hot-reloaded in development
4. **No visibility** — there is no way to see who is being rate limited or how close users are to limits

The event-creation limiter (`rateLimit.server.ts`) allows 10 events per hour per IP. An attacker can create 10 events, wait for a deploy, and create 10 more — indefinitely.

## Requirements

### 1. SQLite-Backed Rate Limit Store
- Create a `RateLimit` table in the Prisma schema:
  ```
  model RateLimit {
    id        String   @id @default(cuid())
    key       String   // composite: "{preset}:{ip}"
    count     Int      @default(1)
    windowStart DateTime
    expiresAt DateTime
    
    @@unique([key])
    @@index([expiresAt])
  }
  ```
- Replace in-memory Maps with SQLite reads/writes
- Use `UPSERT` (Prisma `upsert`) for atomic increment-or-create
- Periodic cleanup via DELETE WHERE expiresAt < now() (can be done in the cron endpoint or a PRAGMA-based approach)

### 2. Preserve Existing API
- Keep the same `checkApiRateLimit()` and `rateLimitResponse()` function signatures
- Keep the same presets (read: 120/min, write: 30/min, auth: 10/min)
- Keep the event-creation limiter (10/hour) but back it with SQLite too
- `resetRateLimitStore()` and `resetApiRateLimitStore()` should still work for tests (truncate the table)

### 3. Performance Considerations
- Rate limit checks happen on every request — must be fast
- SQLite reads are fast (~0.01ms for indexed lookups), so this should not be a bottleneck
- Use a single query per check: `INSERT ... ON CONFLICT UPDATE ... RETURNING count`
- Consider caching the result in-memory for the current request to avoid double-reads

### 4. Cleanup Strategy
- Add cleanup to the existing `/api/cron/reminders.ts` endpoint (or create a dedicated cron)
- Delete expired entries: `DELETE FROM RateLimit WHERE expiresAt < datetime("now")`
- Run every 5-10 minutes to keep the table small

### 5. Observability
- Log rate limit violations at `warn` level with IP, preset, and remaining count
- Add rate limit stats to the admin dashboard (#68): top rate-limited IPs, total blocks per preset

### 6. Test Updates
- Update existing rate limit tests to work with SQLite store
- Add tests for: persistence across "restarts" (re-import module), cleanup of expired entries, concurrent access
- Ensure `resetRateLimitStore()` still works for test isolation

title: feat: Add React component tests with Vitest + Testing Library
description:
## Problem

All 519 tests are API/unit tests against server-side logic. Zero React component tests exist. For a UI-heavy app where `EventPage.tsx` alone has ~20 interactive behaviors (add player, remove player, drag reorder, inline edit, push subscribe, share, claim ownership, toggle settings, etc.), regressions in the UI layer are completely invisible to CI.

## Motivation

- `EventPage.tsx` is 1515 lines with complex interactive state — any refactoring or feature addition risks silent UI breakage
- Components like `TeamPicker`, `PaymentSection`, `PublicGamesPage`, and `HistoryPage` have non-trivial logic (drag-and-drop, filtering, pagination) that is untested
- The TDD discipline documented in AGENTS.md cannot be followed for UI work without component testing infrastructure

## Requirements

### Infrastructure
- Add `@testing-library/react`, `@testing-library/jest-dom`, and `@testing-library/user-event` as dev dependencies
- Configure Vitest with `jsdom` or `happy-dom` environment for component test files
- Set up MUI theme provider wrapper for test renders
- Add component test coverage to the existing coverage thresholds

### Priority Components to Test
1. **EventPage.tsx** — add/remove player, optimistic updates, SSE reconnection, inline editing, ownership controls, quick join
2. **TeamPicker.tsx** — drag-and-drop between teams, team name editing
3. **PaymentSection.tsx** — cost setup, payment status toggling, share recalculation
4. **CreateEventForm.tsx** — form validation, recurrence options, sport selection
5. **PublicGamesPage.tsx** — filtering, view modes, pagination
6. **HistoryPage.tsx** — score editing, ELO display, editable window enforcement

### Test Patterns
- Mock `fetch` calls to API routes, verify correct endpoints and payloads
- Test optimistic update behavior (immediate UI change, revert on error)
- Test SSE event handling (mock EventSource)
- Test i18n rendering (verify translated strings appear)
- Test accessibility: focus management, ARIA attributes, keyboard navigation

### Coverage Target
- Aim for 80%+ branch coverage on all components
- Add component coverage to CI pipeline alongside existing API test coverage

title: feat: Add E2E tests with Playwright for critical user flows
description:
## Problem

Lighthouse CI checks accessibility scores on 3 static pages, but there are no end-to-end tests that exercise actual user flows. The most critical user journeys — create event, add players, randomize teams, record history, manage payments — are completely untested in an integrated way. Unit tests verify individual API routes work, but cannot catch integration failures between the frontend and backend.

## Motivation

- A broken form submission, a mismatched API contract, or a client-side routing issue would pass all 519 current tests
- SSE real-time updates, push notification subscription, and drag-and-drop reordering are inherently integration concerns that unit tests cannot cover
- The recurrence reset (lazy reset on GET) is a critical path that involves complex state transitions — only an E2E test can verify the full cycle

## Requirements

### Infrastructure
- Add Playwright as a dev dependency
- Add `playwright.config.ts` with project configuration
- Create a test database seeding script for E2E scenarios
- Add CI workflow step that starts the dev/preview server and runs Playwright
- Configure screenshot/video capture on failure for debugging

### Critical Flows to Test
1. **Event lifecycle**: Create event → share link → add players → randomize teams → view teams
2. **Authentication**: Sign up → verify email → sign in → quick join as authenticated user
3. **Recurring events**: Create recurring event → trigger reset → verify history snapshot created → verify new date
4. **Player management**: Add player → reorder (drag) → remove player → undo remove → bench promotion
5. **Payments**: Set event cost → verify split calculation → mark payments → verify history snapshot includes payments
6. **Public games**: Create public event → verify it appears on /public → filter by sport
7. **Ownership**: Claim ownerless event → transfer ownership → relinquish ownership
8. **Real-time**: Open event in two tabs → add player in one → verify SSE update in other

### Test Environment
- Use SQLite test database (separate from dev/unit test databases)
- Seed with known test data for deterministic assertions
- Run in CI with headless Chromium
- Consider mobile viewport tests for touch-specific behaviors (team picker)

### CI Integration
- Run after unit tests pass (to avoid slow E2E runs on broken builds)
- Upload Playwright HTML report as CI artifact
- Fail the build on any E2E test failure

title: feat: Structured logging, error tracking, and APM observability
description:
## Problem

Every log in the codebase is a bare `console.log`/`console.warn`/`console.error` with ad-hoc string formatting:
- `[webhook] delivered to ...` (`webhook.server.ts`)
- `[email] Sending verification email to ...` (`email.server.ts`)

There is:
- No structured JSON logging — logs are unqueryable plain text
- No request IDs — impossible to trace a user request through the system
- No error tracking service — production errors are invisible unless you happen to be watching `fly logs`
- No performance monitoring — no visibility into slow queries, slow webhook deliveries, or SSE connection counts
- No log levels — everything goes to stdout with no way to filter

When something breaks in production, the only option is grepping raw text logs on Fly.io.

## Requirements

### 1. Structured Logging with Pino
- Replace all `console.log/warn/error` calls with a structured logger (pino recommended for Node.js performance)
- Log as JSON with fields: `timestamp`, `level`, `msg`, `requestId`, `eventId`, `userId`, `duration`
- Add request ID middleware that generates a UUID per request and threads it through all log calls
- Use child loggers for domain contexts: `logger.child({ module: "webhook" })`, `logger.child({ module: "email" })`

### 2. Error Tracking (Sentry)
- Add Sentry SDK for automatic error capture
- Configure source maps upload in the build step
- Tag errors with: `eventId`, `userId`, `route`, `requestId`
- Set up alerts for new error types and error rate spikes
- Add Sentry to the Astro middleware for automatic request instrumentation

### 3. Key Metrics to Track
- **API latency** per route (p50, p95, p99)
- **SSE connection count** per event and total
- **Webhook delivery success rate** and latency
- **Database query duration** (Prisma query events)
- **Rate limit hits** per preset
- **Push notification delivery success/failure**
- **Recurrence reset events** (how often, duration)

### 4. Log Levels Strategy
- `error`: Unhandled exceptions, failed critical operations (DB write failures, auth failures)
- `warn`: Rate limit hits, webhook delivery failures, geocoding failures
- `info`: Request start/end, player join/leave, game reset, email sent
- `debug`: Full request/response bodies, Prisma queries (dev only)

### 5. Production Log Drain
- Configure Fly.io log shipping to a log aggregation service (Axiom, Datadog, or Grafana Cloud free tier)
- Set up dashboards for: error rate, request latency, active SSE connections, webhook health
- Set up alerts for: error rate spike, high latency, zero SSE connections (indicates broken real-time)

## Migration Path
1. Add pino + pino-pretty (dev) dependencies
2. Create `src/lib/logger.server.ts` with configured logger
3. Replace console calls module by module (start with webhook and email — highest value)
4. Add request ID middleware
5. Add Sentry integration
6. Configure log drain on Fly.io

title: feat: SQLite production hardening — WAL mode, backups via Litestream, busy_timeout
description:
## Problem

The production SQLite database runs on a Fly.io volume (`football_data` mount, 1GB) with a single 256MB machine. There is:
- No backup strategy — if the volume corrupts or the machine dies, all data is permanently lost
- No WAL mode configuration — default journal mode causes readers to block on writes
- No `busy_timeout` — concurrent write attempts fail immediately instead of waiting
- No point-in-time recovery capability

This is the single highest operational risk in the project.

## Requirements

### 1. Enable WAL Mode
- Configure Prisma/SQLite connection to use WAL journal mode
- Add `PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL` and `PRAGMA busy_timeout=5000` on connection init
- This can be done via Prisma `datasource` URL parameters or a connection initialization hook
- WAL allows concurrent readers during writes — critical for SSE connections that read while players are being added

### 2. Litestream Replication
- Add [Litestream](https://litestream.io/) to the Docker image for continuous SQLite replication
- Configure replication to an S3-compatible bucket (Fly.io Tigris, Cloudflare R2, or AWS S3)
- Use Litestream as the process supervisor (it starts the app as a child process)
- Update `Dockerfile` to install Litestream and use it as the entrypoint
- Update `fly.toml` if needed for the new entrypoint
- Add `litestream.yml` configuration file

### 3. Automated Backups
- Litestream provides continuous replication (sub-second RPO)
- Configure retention policy (e.g., keep 7 days of WAL segments)
- Document restore procedure in case of data loss
- Test restore procedure and document it

### 4. SQLite Connection Tuning
- Set `PRAGMA synchronous=NORMAL` (safe with WAL mode, better performance)
- Set `PRAGMA cache_size=-20000` (20MB page cache)
- Set `PRAGMA foreign_keys=ON` (enforce FK constraints)
- Consider `PRAGMA mmap_size` for read performance

### 5. Monitoring
- Add a health check that verifies the database is writable (not just readable)
- Log database file size periodically
- Alert if Litestream replication falls behind

## References
- [Litestream on Fly.io guide](https://litestream.io/guides/fly/)
- [SQLite production checklist](https://kerkour.com/sqlite-for-servers)
- [Fly.io LiteFS alternative](https://fly.io/docs/litefs/) (if multi-region is needed later)

title: feat: Event activity log for full transparency
description:
## Summary

Add an activity log to each event that records every action taken, providing full transparency for the owner (and optionally other participants).

## Motivation

Currently there's no way to know who added/removed a player, who changed the cost, or who marked a payment as paid. This makes it hard for event owners to understand what happened, especially in events with many participants. An activity log solves this by recording every mutation with timestamps and actor information.

## Requirements

### Core
- Record all event mutations as log entries with: timestamp, action type, actor (user name or "anonymous"), and relevant details
- Actions to log:
  - Player added / removed (by whom, which player)
  - Player claimed / unclaimed
  - Payment status changed (who marked whom as paid/exempt/pending)
  - Cost set / updated / removed (old value → new value)
  - Teams randomized / manually edited
  - Team names changed
  - Player order changed / reset
  - Event title / location / sport / visibility changed
  - Ownership claimed / relinquished
  - Recurrence reset triggered
- Owner can toggle whether the log is visible to all participants or only to the owner
- Log should be paginated (events can accumulate hundreds of entries over time)

### Data Model
- New `EventLog` model:
  ```
  model EventLog {
    id        String   @id @default(cuid())
    eventId   String
    event     Event    @relation(...)
    action    String   // e.g. "player_added", "payment_updated", "cost_set"
    actor     String?  // user name or null for anonymous
    actorId   String?  // userId if authenticated
    details   String   // JSON with action-specific data
    createdAt DateTime @default(now())
    @@index([eventId, createdAt])
  }
  ```

### API
- `GET /api/events/[id]/log` — paginated log entries (owner-only, or all if owner enabled visibility)
- New `Event` field: `logPublic Boolean @default(false)` — toggle log visibility
- `PUT /api/events/[id]/log-visibility` — owner toggles log visibility

### UI
- Accessible via a small "Activity" or clock icon button (not prominent)
- Clean timeline/feed UX — each entry shows: relative time, actor, action description
- Color-coded by action type (green for additions, red for removals, blue for changes)
- Filterable by action type
- i18n: action descriptions in all 6 locales

### Non-goals (for now)
- Real-time SSE updates on the log page (can re-fetch on visit)
- Editing or deleting log entries
- Export functionality

## Technical Notes
- Each mutation endpoint needs a one-liner to append a log entry (similar to how `sseManager.broadcast` is called)
- Consider a helper: `logEvent(eventId, action, actor, details)`
- Rate limiting on the GET endpoint to prevent abuse
- Log entries are append-only, never modified or deleted
- Add `EventLog` cleanup to `beforeEach` in tests

## Labels
enhancement, transparency

title: Allow event owners to lock events with a password and/or restrict access to specific users
description:
## Problem

Currently, anyone with the event link can view and join the event. Event owners have no way to restrict access, which can be problematic for private games where only invited players should participate.

## Proposed Solution

Allow event owners to protect their events with two complementary access control mechanisms:

### 1. Password Protection
- Owner can set an optional password on the event
- Visitors without a session/invite must enter the password before viewing or joining
- Password is set/changed/removed from Event Settings
- Once entered correctly, the password is remembered for that browser session (cookie or localStorage)

### 2. User-Based Access (Invite List)
- Owner can share the event with specific authenticated users (by email or username)
- Invited users bypass the password requirement entirely
- Invited users see the event in their dashboard under a new "Invited" section
- Owner can manage the invite list (add/remove users) from Event Settings

### Access Flow
1. User opens event link
2. If user is the owner → full access
3. If user is authenticated and on the invite list → full access (no password needed)
4. If event has a password → show password prompt
5. If no password and no invite list → public access (current behavior)

### UI Changes
- New "Access Control" section in the Event Settings accordion
- Password field with show/hide toggle
- Autocomplete input to invite users by email
- List of invited users with remove button
- Lock icon badge on the event header when protected

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Owner can set, change, and remove a password on an event
- [ ] Visitors must enter the correct password to view a password-protected event
- [ ] Password is remembered per-browser so users do not have to re-enter it on refresh
- [ ] Owner can invite specific users by email
- [ ] Invited users bypass the password requirement
- [ ] Invited users see the event in their dashboard
- [ ] Owner can remove users from the invite list
- [ ] All existing public/ownerless event behavior is preserved when no password or invite list is set
- [ ] Mobile-friendly UI for password prompt and invite management
- [ ] i18n strings added to all 6 locales
- [ ] API rate limiting applied to password attempts
- [ ] Tests cover password verification, invite list CRUD, and access control logic

title: feat: Payment tracking — split the cost and track who paid
description:
## Problem

The biggest pain point for event organizers is managing field payments. After booking and paying for the field, the organizer has to chase players for their share, track who paid and who didn't, and deal with multiple payment methods (cash, Revolut, MB Way, bank transfer, etc.). There's no centralized way to see the payment status at a glance.

## Proposed Solution

A **payment tracking** feature that acts as a logistics tool — not a payment gateway. The organizer sets the total cost, the app calculates each player's share, and payment status is tracked regardless of how players actually pay.

### Core Concept: "Split the Cost"

- Organizer sets the total field cost (e.g. 50 EUR) in a collapsible section on the event page
- App auto-calculates equal shares based on active players (bench players excluded)
- Shares recalculate live as players join/leave
- Organizer (or anyone for ownerless events) marks players as paid/pending/exempt
- Payment method is optional free text (cash, Revolut, MB Way, etc.)
- Organizer can paste payment details (Revolut handle, MB Way number, IBAN) visible to all players

### Data Model

**EventCost** (1:1 with Event):
- `totalAmount: Float` — total field cost
- `currency: String @default("EUR")`
- `paymentDetails: String?` — free text: "Revolut @jose / MB Way 912345678"

**PlayerPayment** (per player per event):
- `playerName: String` — matches Player.name (not FK, survives player deletion)
- `eventCostId: String` — FK to EventCost
- `amount: Float` — calculated share (or custom amount in future)
- `status: String` — "pending" | "paid" | "exempt"
- `method: String?` — free text (cash, revolut, mbway, transfer, other)
- `paidAt: DateTime?`
- `markedBy: String?` — userId who marked it

### History Integration

When a recurring event resets:
- Payment state is snapshotted as JSON into `GameHistory.paymentsSnapshot` (same pattern as `teamsSnapshot`)
- History page shows who paid for each past game
- Payments remain editable in history within the 7-day editable window
- New game occurrence starts fresh with no payments

### Where the UI Appears

1. **EventPage** — collapsible "Split the cost" section below players. Collapsed by default if no cost set. Shows:
   - Total cost + per-player share
   - Payment details (with copy button)
   - Summary: "8/10 paid (40.00 / 50.00 EUR)"
   - Per-player payment status chips (tap to toggle paid/pending)

2. **HistoryPage** — each history card shows payment snapshot below score/teams. Editable within the 7-day window.

### What This Does NOT Do

- No actual payment processing (no Stripe, no Revolut API)
- No automatic payment detection
- No complex splitting rules (the `splitMode` field allows future "custom" amounts per player)

### Why This Works

- Solves the real pain: tracking who paid, not processing payments
- Works with any payment method — cash, Revolut, MB Way, Bizum, bank transfer
- Zero friction: cost section is optional and collapsed by default
- Payment details field is flexible: Revolut link, MB Way number, or "pay João at the field"
- Persists into history so past games show payment records

## Implementation Plan (TDD)

1. Prisma schema migration (EventCost, PlayerPayment, paymentsSnapshot on GameHistory)
2. API: `PUT /api/events/[id]/cost` — set/update event cost
3. API: `GET /api/events/[id]/payments` — list payment statuses
4. API: `PUT /api/events/[id]/payments` — mark player as paid/pending/exempt
5. Snapshot payments on recurrence reset (update `[id]/index.ts`)
6. Include payment snapshot in history API responses
7. Tests for all API routes
8. UI: PaymentSection component for EventPage
9. UI: Payment display in HistoryCardFull
10. i18n strings for all 6 locales (en, pt, es, fr, de, it)

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Organizer can set a total cost and currency for an event
- [ ] Per-player share is auto-calculated from total / active players
- [ ] Shares recalculate when players join or leave
- [ ] Organizer can paste payment details visible to all players
- [ ] Any player can see payment details and copy them
- [ ] Organizer can mark each player as paid, pending, or exempt
- [ ] Payment method can be optionally recorded per player
- [ ] Payment summary shows paid count and amount at a glance
- [ ] Payments are snapshotted into history on recurrence reset
- [ ] History page shows payment data for past games
- [ ] Payments in history are editable within the 7-day window
- [ ] Cost section is optional — events work fine without it
- [ ] All i18n strings added to 6 locales
- [ ] Full test coverage for API routes
- [ ] Rate limiting applied to payment mutation endpoints

title: feat: implement real-time updates via Server-Sent Events (SSE)
description:
## Current State

The project uses SWR with polling (`refreshInterval: 5000`) for data synchronization:

```typescript
// EventPage.tsx
const { data: event } = useSWR<EventData>(
  `/api/events/${eventId}`,
  fetcher,
  { refreshInterval: 5000, revalidateOnFocus: true },
);
```

This means:
- Every 5 seconds, SWR polls the server for updates
- On window focus, it refetches
- **Not truly real-time**, but eventually consistent

## Existing Infrastructure (PR #89)

SSE infrastructure was implemented but **NOT integrated**:

### What exists:
- `src/lib/sse.server.ts` - SSE connection manager with `add/remove/broadcast` functions
- `src/pages/api/events/[id]/stream.ts` - SSE endpoint for client subscriptions
- Tests pass for the SSE manager

### What's missing:
- ❌ No client subscribes to the EventSource
- ❌ No mutation endpoints broadcast updates via `sseManager.broadcast()`

## Implementation Required

### 1. Client-side EventSource Subscription

Add to `EventPage.tsx`:

```typescript
useEffect(() => {
  const es =new EventSource(`/api/events/${eventId}/stream`);
  es.onmessage = (e) => {
    const { event, data } = JSON.parse(e.data);
    if (event === 'update') mutate();
  };
  return () => es.close();
}, [eventId, mutate]);
```

### 2. Server-side Broadcasts

Add `sseManager.broadcast()` calls to all mutation endpoints after successful operations:

| Endpoint | File | Action |
|----------|------|--------|
| Add Player | `players.ts` (POST) | Broadcast after player added |
| Remove Player | `players.ts` (DELETE) | Broadcast after player removed |
| Randomize Teams | `randomize.ts` | Broadcast after teams shuffled |
| Reset Player Order | `reset-player-order.ts` | Broadcast after order reset |
| Reorder Players | `reorder-players.ts` | Broadcast after manual reorder |
| Claim Ownership | `claim.ts` | Broadcast after ownership change |
| Claim Player | `claim-player.ts` | Broadcast after player claimed |
| Update Teams | `teams.ts` | Broadcast after team changes |
| Update Settings | Various | Broadcast after title, location, etc. |

### 3. Remove Polling

Replace `refreshInterval: 5000` with SSE-driven updates:
- Keep `revalidateOnFocus: true` for resilience
- SWR will still handle caching and error states

### 4. Testing

- Add tests for SSE broadcast behavior
- Test multi-client synchronization
- Test reconnection scenarios

## Benefits

- **Instant updates** - Changes appear immediately without 5-second delay
- **Reduced server load** - No more polling every 5 seconds per connected client
- **Better UX** - Real-time collaboration feels more responsive

## Related

- PR #89 - Added SSE infrastructure
- Issue #66 - Original real-time feature request

title: feat: Comprehensive Notification Settings & Email Enablement
description:
## Overview

Users need full control over their notification preferences. Currently:
- Email functions exist (`sendGameInvite`, `sendReminder`, `sendWeeklySummary`) but are **not wired up**
- Cron job only sends push notifications, not emails
- No user-facing notification preferences UI
- Issue #79 partially covers management but lacks implementation details

## Requirements

### 1. Enable Email Notifications
Wire up existing email functions to actual triggers:

#### Game Invites
- [ ] Call `sendGameInvite` when owner adds player to event
- [ ] Include event title, date/time, location, and join link
- [ ] Respect user's notification preferences

#### Game Reminders
- [ ] Update `/api/cron/reminders` to send emails in addition to push
- [ ] Support configurable reminder times (24h, 2h, 1h)
- [ ] Use`sendReminder` from`email.server.ts`

#### Weekly Summaries
- [ ] Create new cron endpoint for weekly summaries
- [ ] Call `sendWeeklySummary` for opted-in users
- [ ] Include upcoming games, recent results

### 2. Notification Preferences Database Schema
Add`NotificationPreferences` model:

```prisma
model NotificationPreferences {
  id                String   @id @default(cuid())
  userId            String   @unique
  user              User     @relation(fields: [userId])
  
  // Global toggles
  emailEnabled      Boolean  @default(true)
  pushEnabled       Boolean  @default(true)
  
  // Per-type toggles
  gameInviteEmail       Boolean  @default(true)
  gameInvitePush        Boolean  @default(true)
  gameReminderEmail     Boolean  @default(true)
  gameReminderPush      Boolean  @default(true)
  weeklySummaryEmail    Boolean  @default(false)
  
  // Reminder preferences
  reminder24h      Boolean  @default(true)
  reminder2h       Boolean  @default(true)
  reminder1h       Boolean  @default(false)
  
  createdAt        DateTime @default(now())
  updatedAt        DateTime @updatedAt
}
```

### 3. Notification Settings Page
Create `/dashboard/settings/notifications` route with:

- **Email notifications** toggle (master switch)
- **Push notifications** toggle (master switch)
- **Game invites**: Email / Push toggles
- **Game reminders**: Email / Push / Time preferences (24h, 2h, 1h checkboxes)
- **Weekly summary**: Email toggle + frequency selector
- **Per-event override**: Unsubscribe from specific events

### 4. API Endpoints

- [ ] `GET /api/user/notification-preferences` - Get user's preferences
- [ ] `PUT /api/user/notification-preferences` - Update preferences
- [ ] Add`?unsubscribe=...` param to email links for one-click unsubscribe

### 5. Cron Jobs (Fly.io)

- [ ] `/api/cron/reminders` - Send 24h/2h/1h reminders (push + email)
- [ ] `/api/cron/weekly-summary` - Send weekly summaries (email only)

### 6. Email Improvements

- [ ] Add unsubscribe link to all email templates
- [ ] Add`preferencesUrl` to email templates linking to settings page
- [ ] Handle `Notification-Preferences` header for ESP compliance

## Technical Notes

- Existing email functions in `src/lib/email.server.ts`are ready to use
- Resend API key already configured in production
- Push notifications via `sendPushToEvent` already working
- Need to add preferences table to Prisma schema

## Database Migration

```bash
npx prisma migrate dev --name add_notification_preferences
```

## Testing

- [ ] Unit tests for preference toggles
- [ ] Integration tests for email triggers
- [ ] E2E tests for settings page
- [ ] Verify unsubscribe links work

## Priority

**Phase 2 — User Experience Polish**

## Related Issues

- #55 (closed) - Email notifications (functions implemented)
- #50 (closed) - Scheduled reminders (push only)
- #79 - Allow to disable and manage notifications

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Users can toggle email/push notifications globally
- [ ] Users can configure reminder times (24h, 2h, 1h)
- [ ] Weekly summary emails are sent to opted-in users
- [ ] Game invite emails are sent when owner adds players
- [ ] Reminders include both push and email channels
- [ ] One-click unsubscribe works from emails
- [ ] All preferences persist and are respected

title: Improve UX for Event Owners: Reorganize Event Management Options
description:
## Problem

Event owners currently see all event management options mangled together in one view, making it difficult to find relevant settings quickly and overwhelming for users.

## Proposed Solution

Reorganize event management UI with progressive disclosure:

### Quick Actions (Visible by Default)
- Edit event details
- View/check-in participants
- Send notifications
- Check event status

### Advanced Settings (Collapsed or Separate Page)
- Event visibility settings
- Participant limits
- Custom registration fields
- Event branding options
- Integration settings

### When Needed Approach
- Show contextual actions based on event state (e.g., "Start Event" only when event hasn't started)
- Hide completed actions (e.g., don't show "Setup" after event is configured)
- Use badges for pending actions requiring attention

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Quick actions are immediately visible and identifiable
- [ ] Advanced settings are accessible but not cluttering main view
- [ ] Actions are contextual to event lifecycle stage
- [ ] Mobile-friendly layout maintained
- [ ] All existing functionality preserved

title: feat: Enable user data export (GDPR compliance)
description:
## Feature Request

Allow authenticated users to export all their personal data in a machine-readable format (GDPR Right to Data Portability - Article 20).

## Motivation

Under GDPR, users have the right to:
- Receive their personal data in a structured, commonly used format
- Transmit data to another controller without hindrance
- Obtain data in a format that allows further use

## User Stories

1. As a user, I want to download all my personal data
2. As a user, I want my data in a standard format (JSON/CSV)
3. As a user, I want to understand what data is stored about me
4. As a user, I want to port my data to another service

## Data to Export

### Personal Information
- [ ] User ID
- [ ] Name
- [ ] Email address
- [ ] Account creation date
- [ ] Last login date

### Events Created
- [ ] Event IDs
- [ ] Event titles
- [ ] Locations
- [ ] Date/time
- [ ] Team names
- [ ] Public/private status
- [ ] Sport type

### Player Records
- [ ] Player IDs
- [ ] Player names linked to account
- [ ] Events participated in
- [ ] Team assignments

### Game History
- [ ] Historical game records
- [ ] Scores recorded
- [ ] Teams played for

### Settings & Preferences
- [ ] Language preference
- [ ] Theme preference (dark/light)
- [ ] Notification settings

### Authentication Data
- [ ] Linked accounts (Google, etc.)
- [ ] Session information (anonymized)

## Proposed Solution

### API Endpoints

#### GET /api/users/[id]/export

```typescript
// Response - JSON format
{
  user: {
    id: string,
    name: string,
    email: string,
    createdAt: Date,
    updatedAt: Date,
  },
  events: [{
    id: string,
    title: string,
    location: string,
    dateTime: Date,
    teamOneName: string,
    teamTwoName: string,
    sport: string,
    isPublic: boolean,
    createdAt: Date,
  }],
  players: [{
    id: string,
    name: string,
    eventId: string,
    eventName: string,
    teamName: string,
```
